sources.summary: The paper detects significant perpendicular and southeast extended X-ray emission around 3CR 430, interpreted as possible ICM from merging galaxy clusters.

quotes.quote: 3CR 430 ( z=0.0555 ). We detected significant emission perpendicular to the radio axis (above 5 level of confidence) that could be due to ICM from a galaxy cluster at the same position as the radio galaxy. We additionally detected extended X-ray emission at 118 arcsec (128 kpc at the source redshift) above 5 confidence level to the south-east of this source that could hint at the presence of another galaxy cluster, possibly undergoing a merging process with the former. Assuming this emission comes from ICM from a galaxy cluster, the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) would be J211836.50+604704.2 whose redsift is z = 0.05593 0.00026 , which supports the hypothesis of these two galaxy clusters undergoing a merging process. The left panel of Fig. fig:cluster2 shows an optical image of the field of 3CR 430 with radio contours (at 4.9 GHz) and X-ray contours (in the 0.5 - 3 keV band).

gmw_00006547.name: 3CR 458
gmw_00006547.overview: 3CR 458 is a Fanaroff-Riley class II, high-excitation radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.289 [arXiv:1903.10768, arXiv:2011.04668]. It features ionized gas extending over 100 kiloparsecs in clumpy, elongated structures [arXiv:1903.10768], and exhibits extended X-ray emission to its northwest [arXiv:2011.04668].

sources.summary: The paper detects northwest extended X-ray emission near 3CR 458 and weighs whether it is related to a cluster at the radio galaxy redshift.

quotes.quote: 3CR 458 ( z=0.289 ). We detected extended X-ray emission 105 arcsec (460 kpc at the source redshift) above a 5 confidence level to the north-west of this source that could be due to the presence of another galaxy cluster maybe related to the radio galaxy (see Fig. fig:sample ). Wen2015 reported the presence of a galaxy cluster at z=0.407 at the position of the extended emission, which would mean that this extended emission would be unrelated to the radio galaxy (at z=0.289 ). However, there is also another galaxy for which the photometric redshift is known at the position of this extended emission ( Gorshkov1995 ). This galaxy, at z=0.29 , would be consistent with a source belonging to the same galaxy cluster as 3CR 458.

sources.summary: MUSE detects 3C 458 ionized gas more than 100 kpc away in clumpy elongated structures with a complex velocity field.

quotes.quote: 3C 458: FR II/HEG, 1 = 4.38 kpc. Ionized gas is detected at a distance of more than 100 kpc. It is located in various clumps forming various elongated structures, in a general NE-SW direction. The gas velocity field is also rather complex: although there is a tendency for the gas located in the NE quadrant to be generally redshifted (and to be blue-shifted in the SW one) there are changes of speed occurring on small scale.

gmw_00006548.name: Arp 127
gmw_00006548.overview: Arp 127 is an interacting binary galaxy system located at a redshift of approximately 6150 km/s [arXiv:astro-ph/0306362]. The system is composed of the spiral galaxy NGC 0191, which exhibits tidal distortion, and the lenticular galaxy IC 1563 [arXiv:astro-ph/0306362].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Arp 127 as an interacting binary system at about 6150 km/s, resolving a redshift discrepancy between NGC0191, IC1563, and a compact interloper.

quotes.quote: The Arp 127 pair (Arp 1966) is made by NGC0191, a spiral classified as SAB(rs): pec, and IC1563, an S0 pec sp. A third, more compact object is seen among the two galaxies (see fig:arp ). According to the literature, the redshifts of NGC0191 and IC1563 are respectively v=5065 141 km/s and v=13652 141 km/s (Huchra et al. 1993), a surprising difference given the apparent signs of interactions; for this reason IC1563 was not included in our final catalogue, even if according to the quoted redshift and the apparent magnitude ( m = 14.74 ) it should be considered a VLG.

Our measurements for NGC0191 and IC1563 are reported in table A.1, and show that there is no discrepancy: the binary system Arp 127 is at z 6150 km/s, and the two galaxies have a velocity difference of only 60 km/s --, i.e. they have the same velocity taking into account the errors. These two galaxies are clearly interacting, as shown by the tidal distortion in NGC0191.

gmw_00006549.name: NGC0191
gmw_00006549.overview: NGC0191 is a spiral galaxy classified as SAB(rs): pec [arXiv:astro-ph/0306362]. It forms the interacting binary system Arp 127 with the S0 galaxy IC1563, showing clear tidal distortion from this interaction [arXiv:astro-ph/0306362].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC0191 as the spiral member of Arp 127, sharing IC1563's revised velocity and showing tidal distortion that indicates interaction.

quotes.quote: The Arp 127 pair (Arp 1966) is made by NGC0191, a spiral classified as SAB(rs): pec, and IC1563, an S0 pec sp. A third, more compact object is seen among the two galaxies (see fig:arp ). According to the literature, the redshifts of NGC0191 and IC1563 are respectively v=5065 141 km/s and v=13652 141 km/s (Huchra et al. 1993), a surprising difference given the apparent signs of interactions; for this reason IC1563 was not included in our final catalogue, even if according to the quoted redshift and the apparent magnitude ( m = 14.74 ) it should be considered a VLG.

Our measurements for NGC0191 and IC1563 are reported in table A.1, and show that there is no discrepancy: the binary system Arp 127 is at z 6150 km/s, and the two galaxies have a velocity difference of only 60 km/s --, i.e. they have the same velocity taking into account the errors. These two galaxies are clearly interacting, as shown by the tidal distortion in NGC0191.

gmw_00006550.name: IC1563
gmw_00006550.overview: IC1563 is a lenticular galaxy classified as an S0 pec sp [arXiv:astro-ph/0306362]. It forms the interacting galaxy pair Arp 127 with the spiral galaxy NGC0191, sharing a similar velocity with its companion [arXiv:astro-ph/0306362].

sources.summary: The paper remeasures IC1563 as an S0 member of Arp 127 at the same velocity as NGC0191, making it a normal M* interacting galaxy rather than a VLG.

quotes.quote: The Arp 127 pair (Arp 1966) is made by NGC0191, a spiral classified as SAB(rs): pec, and IC1563, an S0 pec sp. A third, more compact object is seen among the two galaxies (see fig:arp ). According to the literature, the redshifts of NGC0191 and IC1563 are respectively v=5065 141 km/s and v=13652 141 km/s (Huchra et al. 1993), a surprising difference given the apparent signs of interactions; for this reason IC1563 was not included in our final catalogue, even if according to the quoted redshift and the apparent magnitude ( m = 14.74 ) it should be considered a VLG.

Our measurements for NGC0191 and IC1563 are reported in table A.1, and show that there is no discrepancy: the binary system Arp 127 is at z 6150 km/s, and the two galaxies have a velocity difference of only 60 km/s --, i.e. they have the same velocity taking into account the errors.
The new redshift for IC1563 means that this galaxy has M=-19.2 , i.e. it is a typical M^* galaxy and not a VLG.

gmw_00006551.name: NGC 5278/9
gmw_00006551.overview: NGC 5278/9 is a interacting galaxy pair containing two low-luminosity active galactic nuclei [arXiv:1711.09936]. The system features extensive line-emitting filaments and plumes, with spectroscopy indicating the presence of AGN-ionized gas across the pair [arXiv:1711.09936].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 5278/9 as a dual low-luminosity AGN pair with line-emitting filaments, AGN-ionized gas, and possible cross-ionization or fading.

quotes.quote: NGC 5278/9 : This pair has two low-luminosity AGN, near the Sy 2/LINER boundary in line-ratio diagrams depending on aperture size. The system shows extensive filaments or plumes in line emission, some almost radial ( KKHH89 , Fig. fig-ngc5279 ). Our spectroscopy in the BPT diagram shows AGN-ionized gas across the pair, including one of these plumes to the east.

This may suggest that either the AGN are heavily obscured along our line of sight or have faded over the light-travel time from the nucleus to the extended emission regions. Because gas in each disc may be largely shielded from its own AGN, this pair is a potential instance of cross-ionization. In this system, we have the data to estimate both the nuclear luminosities and the ionizing-flux requirement at various points in the extended emission, to see whether the nuclear output has changed dramatically over the relevant light-travel time, and how important obscuration of the AGN is along our line of sight.

gmw_00006552.name: Bootes15
gmw_00006552.overview: Bootes15 is a 500 micron riser galaxy that is classified as a starburst and infrared galaxy [arXiv:2203.01049]. High-resolution observations resolve the system into two distinct sources with extended emission [arXiv:2203.01049].

sources.summary: The paper follows up Bootes15 as a 500 micron riser, finding it resolves into two SMA sources with extended SCUBA-2 emission, though its photometric redshift remains poorly constrained.

quotes.quote: We selected the three brightest sources at 500 m from the twelve sources in the G20 sample without SMA counterparts (Bootes15, Bootes24 and Bootes27) for multi-wavelength follow-up observations. An additional 500 m riser (XMM-M5) was observed as part of a separate SMA project, but will also be presented in this paper. The positions and Herschel SPIRE photometry for these sources are presented in Table table: integrated_fir , and in Figure fig: sample_colours we show their Herschel SPIRE colours. We find that our 500 m risers are quite typical of the majority of the global 500 m riser population, although potentially residing towards the less extreme end.

We find evidence of multiplicity in three of our four 500 m risers, with Bootes15, Bootes24 and Bootes27 each breaking up into two resolved sources in the high resolution SMA maps, and all three showing extended emission in the SCUBA-2 maps. This builds on the work of G20, who found that 35 of bright ( S_ 500 > 60 mJy) 500 m risers are multiples, while fainter 500 m risers are also blends more often than expected.
We find that the photometric redshifts for Bootes15, Bootes15.SCUBA1 and Bootes15.SCUBA2 are poorly constrained due to poor quality photometry.

gmw_00006553.name: Bootes24
gmw_00006553.overview: Bootes24 is a high-redshift starburst galaxy selected as a 500 micron riser [arXiv:2203.01049]. High-resolution observations resolve the system into two distinct sources with extended submillimeter emission, likely residing at a redshift of z ~ 2 to 3 [arXiv:2203.01049].

sources.summary: The paper follows up Bootes24 as a 500 micron riser, resolving it into two SMA sources, detecting extended SCUBA-2 emission, and estimating redshifts around z 2-3.

quotes.quote: We selected the three brightest sources at 500 m from the twelve sources in the G20 sample without SMA counterparts (Bootes15, Bootes24 and Bootes27) for multi-wavelength follow-up observations. An additional 500 m riser (XMM-M5) was observed as part of a separate SMA project, but will also be presented in this paper. The positions and Herschel SPIRE photometry for these sources are presented in Table table: integrated_fir , and in Figure fig: sample_colours we show their Herschel SPIRE colours. We find that our 500 m risers are quite typical of the majority of the global 500 m riser population, although potentially residing towards the less extreme end.

We find evidence of multiplicity in three of our four 500 m risers, with Bootes15, Bootes24 and Bootes27 each breaking up into two resolved sources in the high resolution SMA maps, and all three showing extended emission in the SCUBA-2 maps. This builds on the work of G20, who found that 35 of bright ( S_ 500 > 60 mJy) 500 m risers are multiples, while fainter 500 m risers are also blends more often than expected.

By comparison, we find that the photometric redshift PDFs for the sources in the Bootes24 field are all well constrained, likely residing at redshifts of z 2 - 3 , with Bootes24 itself being somewhat broader with a higher redshift tail to its PDF. For Bootes27 and XMM-M5, the photometric redshift PDFs are similarly well constrained, but the sources are spread over a wider range of redshifts, with two of the SCUBA-2 sources likely residing at z 2 , one likely residing at z 4 , and XMM-M5 itself straddling these two redshifts. For Bootes24, Bootes27 and XMM-M5, these results are in good agreement with the FIR/sub-mm colours shown in Figure fig: colours .

gmw_00006554.name: Bootes27
gmw_00006554.overview: Bootes27 is a high-redshift starburst galaxy originally identified as a 500 micron riser [arXiv:2203.01049]. High-resolution observations resolve the system into two distinct sources, which exhibit extended emission and may be blended with a nearby source [arXiv:2203.01049].

sources.summary: The paper follows up Bootes27 as a 500 micron riser, resolving it into two SMA sources, detecting extended SCUBA-2 emission, and finding nearby sources may make it artificially red.

quotes.quote: We selected the three brightest sources at 500 m from the twelve sources in the G20 sample without SMA counterparts (Bootes15, Bootes24 and Bootes27) for multi-wavelength follow-up observations. An additional 500 m riser (XMM-M5) was observed as part of a separate SMA project, but will also be presented in this paper. The positions and Herschel SPIRE photometry for these sources are presented in Table table: integrated_fir , and in Figure fig: sample_colours we show their Herschel SPIRE colours. We find that our 500 m risers are quite typical of the majority of the global 500 m riser population, although potentially residing towards the less extreme end.

We find evidence of multiplicity in three of our four 500 m risers, with Bootes15, Bootes24 and Bootes27 each breaking up into two resolved sources in the high resolution SMA maps, and all three showing extended emission in the SCUBA-2 maps. This builds on the work of G20, who found that 35 of bright ( S_ 500 > 60 mJy) 500 m risers are multiples, while fainter 500 m risers are also blends more often than expected.

Finally, we note that, for Bootes27, one of the SNR > 4 SCUBA detections lies within 31^ of the Bootes27 500 m riser, placing it just within the FWHM of the Herschel SPIRE beamsize at 500 m ( 35.2^ ) but outside of the 250 m ( 17.6^ ) and 350 m ( 23.9^ ) beamsize. This SCUBA-2 source does not have a separate counterpart in the HerMES catalogues, indicating that it may contribute to making Bootes27 artificially red.

gmw_00006555.name: XMM-M5
gmw_00006555.overview: XMM-M5 is a high-redshift starburst galaxy classified as a 500-micron riser [arXiv:2203.01049]. It resides in a field that is plausibly overdense with far-infrared and submillimeter-bright sources [arXiv:2203.01049].

sources.summary: The paper includes XMM-M5 as a 500 micron riser with SMA and SCUBA-2 follow-up, finding an overdense field and redshift estimates split between z 1.5-2 and z 4 components.

quotes.quote: An additional 500 m riser (XMM-M5) was observed as part of a separate SMA project, but will also be presented in this paper. The positions and Herschel SPIRE photometry for these sources are presented in Table table: integrated_fir , and in Figure fig: sample_colours we show their Herschel SPIRE colours. We find that our 500 m risers are quite typical of the majority of the global 500 m riser population, although potentially residing towards the less extreme end.

The XMM-M5 field is plausibly overdense in FIR/sub-mm bright sources, and their colours suggest that they all reside at z 2 . The MMpz results, however, offer a somewhat more complicated picture, placing two of the SCUBA-2 detections at z 1.5 - 2 and the third (XMM-M5.SCUBA1) at z 4 . This is consistent with the Herschel SPIRE photometry for XMM-M5.SCUBA1 which satisfies the colour criteria for a 500 m riser (see Table tab: scuba_integrated_fir ).

For Bootes27 and XMM-M5, the photometric redshift PDFs are similarly well constrained, but the sources are spread over a wider range of redshifts, with two of the SCUBA-2 sources likely residing at z 2 , one likely residing at z 4 , and XMM-M5 itself straddling these two redshifts. For Bootes24, Bootes27 and XMM-M5, these results are in good agreement with the FIR/sub-mm colours shown in Figure fig: colours .

sources.summary: The paper treats the M05 source as a z=5.70, M_1450 23.6 quasar and uses its faint optical luminosity and survey surface density to constrain the faint-end high-redshift QSO luminosity function.

quotes.quote: Recently, Mahabal (2005, M05 hereafter) in the field around the z=6.42 quasar SDSS J1148+5251 discovered a quasar at redshift z=5.70 with luminosity of M_ 1450 23.6 , i.e. z _ AB =23.0 . This quasar is the faintest known optically selected quasar at z>5.5 , allowing determination of the faint end of the quasar LF to fainter luminosities. The authors claim the source, identified in a 2.5 deg ^2 survey, has a surface density consistent with the Wolf (2003) LF extrapolated through a PDE within their effective volume, which follows the R band drop-out criterion of Stern (2000), i.e. 4.3 z 5.8 .

They found a faint AGN at z=5.19 with z _ AB =23.9 , corresponding to an absolute magnitude of M_ 1450 22.8 , i.e. nearly a magnitude fainter than the M05 source, showing the power of X-ray observations.

gmw_00006556.name: GH 47
gmw_00006556.overview: GH 47 is a radio-quiet dwarf galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2211.00050]. Its X-ray spectrum resembles that of a Type II active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1411.2902], and it exhibits low soft-band variability consistent with scattered emission [arXiv:1411.2902].

sources.summary: The paper finds GH 47 has a Type II AGN-like X-ray spectrum, poor simple-model fits, and low soft-band variability consistent with scattered emission.

quotes.quote: GH 47 and GH 94 had a different spectral shape than all the other objects, with a spectral shape resembling that of Type II AGN. GH 47 and GH 94 were omitted from the spectral fit tables since their spectra cannot be described properly by the same models as the Type I objects. Type II objects As mentioned above, fits to GH 47 and GH 94 show different spectral shapes compared to the other objects. In the case of GH 47, both a simple power-law and a power-law plus blackbody model produce extremely poor fits (reduced- ^2 > 4.1 in both cases).

Finally, we consider the variability in the type-II object, GH 47. We would expect the variability of the soft emission (dominated by the scattered power-law component) to have little variability, and that is what we find. In Figure fig:lc_gh47 we show the 0.2 -- 2 keV lightcurve of GH 47. The normalized excess variance across the 0.2 -- 10 keV band (in order to compare to the other GH objects) is ^2_ NXS = - 2.9 , significantly smaller than all the other GH objects, as would be expected if it is a type II object.

sources.summary: The paper treats GH047 as a radio-quiet AGN-hosting dwarf galaxy with a potential IMBH, using VLBA and VLA observations to probe compact and extended accretion-outflow emission.

quotes.quote: The above mechanisms of emission and evolution from compact to large scale (pc - kpc) structure are investigated through a set of radio observations spanning multiple resolutions (and physical scales) involving four potential IMBH hosting AGNs. This includes our VLBA L-band (1.4 GHz) high-resolution observations (milli-arcsecond scale; probing the few to tens of pc) conducted in 2021, and archival VLA A-array L and X-band (1.5 GHz and 9 GHz respectively) intermediate to larger resolution observations (sub-arcsec to arcsec scales; probing hundred pc to kpc). At the VLA scales, the source is compact and unresolved, though with a dominant emission. At the VLBA scales, the sources become marginally resolved to allow the discerning of extensions from the compact emission regions.

gmw_00006557.name: Pisces VII/Triangulum (Tri) III
aliases_external: Pisces VII Dwarf / Triangulum III Dwarf
gmw_00006557.overview: Pisces VII/Triangulum III is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy located in the Local Group [arXiv:2104.03859, arXiv:2305.13966]. Deep imaging of its horizontal branch places it at a distance of approximately 1.0 Mpc, making it a highly probable satellite of the Triangulum Galaxy [arXiv:2305.13966].

sources.summary: The paper reports Pisces VII/Triangulum (Tri) III as an ultra-faint dwarf near M33, with uncertain distance, low luminosity, and possible status as an M33 satellite or isolated field dwarf.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We report deep imaging observations with DOLoRes@TNG of an ultra-faint dwarf satellite candidate of the Triangulum galaxy (M33) found by visual inspection of the public imaging data release of the DESI Legacy Imaging Surveys. Pisces VII/Triangulum (Tri) III is found at a projected distance of 72 kpc from M33, and using the tip of the red giant branch method we estimate a distance of D=1.0^ +0.3 _ 0.2 Mpc , meaning the galaxy could either be an isolated ultra-faint or the second known satellite of M33. We estimate an absolute magnitude of M_V=-6.1 0.2 if Pisces VII/Tri II is at the distance of M33, or as bright as M_V=-6.8 0.2 if the galaxy is isolated. At the isolated distance, it has a physical half-light radius of r_ h =131 61 pc consistent with similarly faint galaxies around the Milky Way. As the tip of the red giant branch is sparsely populated, constraining a precision distance is not possible, but if Pisces VII/Tri III can be confirmed as a true satellite of M33 it is a significant finding.

Overall, this approach appears to favour an isolated distance for Pisces VII/Tri III. For the rest of the paper, we assume a distance for the dwarf of D=1.0^ +0.3 _ 0.2 Mpc, and use these to calculate a physical size and luminosity range for this candidate. It is only the second potential satellite galaxy of M33 discovered.

sources.summary: The paper remeasures Pisces VII/Triangulum III with deep Gemini imaging, resolving its horizontal branch, confirming it as a likely M33 satellite dwarf, and noting possible young stars.

quotes.quote: Pisces VII/Triangulum III ( ) was discovered in the DESI Legacy Imaging Survey and was shown to be a Local Group dwarf galaxy with follow-up imaging from the 4-m Telescopio Nazionale Galileo. However, this imaging was unable to reach the horizontal branch of , preventing a precision distance measurement. The distance bound from the red giant branch population placed \ as either an isolated ultra-faint dwarf galaxy or the second known satellite galaxy of Triangulum (M33). Using deep imaging from Gemini GMOS-N, we have resolved the horizontal branch of , and measure a distance of , making \ a likely satellite of M33.

Based on its size and luminosity, we conclude \ is a highly probable dwarf galaxy. Our deep data also allows us to determine the distance to \ from its horizontal branch of . This locates it at a 3D distance of within the halo of M33 making \ a highly probable satellite of this system. \ is only the second known satellite of M33 and its location at a large distance from M33 may favour a first infall scenario for the orbital history of M33.

gmw_00006558.name: G10/G11
aliases_external: G10/G11 interacting galaxy pair in the PKS0454-22 field
gmw_00006558.overview: G10/G11 is an interacting galaxy pair with a projected separation of 6.4 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2106.03858]. The pair is associated with the nebula N2 and may be experiencing ram pressure stripping as it falls into a quasar host group [arXiv:2106.03858].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G10/G11 as an interacting galaxy pair associated with nebula N2, likely producing stripped interstellar gas through interaction and infall into the quasar host group.

quotes.quote: N2 is spatially coincident with G10/G11/G13/G14, but with v +1330 \ km \ s^ 1 and 120 \ km \ s^ 1 , only G10 and G11 are kinematically coincident. This kinematic correspondence suggests that N2 arises from gas associated with G10 and G11, which are separated by 6.4 \ pkpc in projection. G10 and G11 may be interacting with one another while also falling into the quasar host group. black Although the morphology is hard to disentangle for G10 and G11, the main reason to think they are interacting is the kinematic and spatial coincidence of these two galaxies, and association with N2.

In particular, N1 spatially and kinematically surrounds black Host/G1/G2/G3/G4/G5 while N2 spatially and kinematically surrounds the interacting galaxy pair G10/G11. Morphologically, N2 has a head-tail structure extending d 85 \ pkpc to the northeast, and a velocity gradient with v +1500 \ km \ s^ 1 at the head of N2 and v +800 \ km \ s^ 1 at the tail. This spatial and kinematic extension is suggestive of ram pressure stripping that would result if G10 and G11 are moving through black the hot halo associated with the galaxy over-density.

From these galaxies, we found an interacting galaxy group ( black Host/G1/G2/G3/G4/G5 , see Figure fig:HST_image ) with a projected separation of 37.4 \ pkpc and an interacting galaxy pair (G10/G11, see Figure fig:HST_image ) with a projected separation of 6.4 \ pkpc .

gmw_00006559.name: CIG 638
gmw_00006559.overview: CIG 638 is an isolated star-forming galaxy [arXiv:1403.1763]. It exhibits a high ratio of CCH relative to HCN, HCO+, and HNC, which suggests a lower fraction of dense molecular gas and a less perturbed interstellar medium [arXiv:1403.1763].

sources.summary: The paper studies CIG 638 as an isolated Sbc galaxy with unusual molecular line ratios, interpreting its high CCH and weak dense-gas tracers as signs of a less perturbed ISM.

quotes.quote: We present 3 mm broad band observations of the galaxy CIG 638, extracted from the AMIGA sample of isolated galaxies. The emission of the J=1-0 transitions of CCH, HCN, HCO ^+ , and HNC are detected. Integrated intensity ratios between these line are compared with similar observations from the literature towards active galaxies including starburst galaxies (SB), active galactic nuclei (AGN), luminous infrared galaxies (LIRG), and GMCs in M33.

A significantly high ratio of CCH with respect to HCN, HCO ^+ , and HNC is found towards CIG 638 when compared with all other galaxies where these species have been detected. This points to either an overabundance of CCH or to a relative lack of dense molecular gas as supported by the low HCN/CO ratio, or both. The data suggest that the CIG 638 is naturally a less perturbed galaxy where a lower fraction of dense molecular gas, as well as a more even distribution could explain the measured ratios. In this scenario the dense gas tracers would be naturally dimmer, while the UV enhanced CCH, would be overproduced in a less shielded medium.

The star-forming galaxy CIG 638 (NGC 5690) matches the isolation criteria from Verley2007b based on revised local number density and tidal parameters (Fig. fig.isolation ) from Argudo-Fernandez2013 . A revision of isolation using spectroscopic data shows CIG 638 is isolated from similar luminosity neighbours, unlike the comparison galaxies used in this paper, for which data exist for evaluating isolation.

gmw_00006560.name: J1423+3529
gmw_00006560.overview: J1423+3529 is a giant radio galaxy with a linear size of 3.4 Mpc [arXiv:2207.01648]. It is a restarted source featuring an inner asymmetric double structure and disrupted, generally straight outer lobes [arXiv:2207.01648].

sources.summary: The appendix describes J1423+3529 as the sample second-largest GRG, with restarted inner structure, disrupted straight outer lobes, and only a faint VLASS host detection.

quotes.quote: J1423+3529 has the second-largest linear size (3.4 Mpc) in our sample. It is a restarted source with an inner asymmetric double of LAS 1.6 ' . The 6 '' BLDF image shows rather disrupted, and generally straight outer lobes, but the LoTSS-DR2 low-resolution image leaves no doubt that the source is a physical entity. Only the host is detected as a faint point source in VLASS.

gmw_00006561.name: J1424+3609
gmw_00006561.overview: J1424+3609 is a very large and straight Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy [arXiv:2207.01648]. It features clear diffuse emission trailing from its lobes, a single hotspot in the northern lobe, and a likely jet knot to the south [arXiv:2207.01648].

sources.summary: The appendix presents J1424+3609 as a very large straight FR II source with trailing diffuse lobe emission, one VLASS hotspot, and a likely jet knot.

quotes.quote: J1424+3609 is a very large and straight FR II source with clear diffuse emission trailing back from the outer extremities of the NNE and SSW lobes towards the host. Only the NNE lobe shows a hotspot in VLASS while the end of SSW lobe is diffuse and of very low surface brightness in VLASS. A compact radio source 20 '' SSW of the host has no optical or IR counterpart and is likely a knot of the SSW jet. The source was first noticed by us in .

gmw_00006562.name: J1427+3328C
gmw_00006562.overview: J1427+3328C is a candidate giant radio galaxy characterized by very inflated, low-surface-brightness lobes [arXiv:2207.01648]. Its proposed host galaxy, 2MASX J14273770+3328081, is the brightest optical and mid-infrared source in the region but lacks detected radio emission [arXiv:2207.01648].

sources.summary: The appendix treats J1427+3328C as a candidate GRG with inflated low-surface-brightness lobes, uncertain flux, and a proposed bright optical/MIR host.

quotes.quote: J1427+3328C: the source with the largest angular size in our sample with very inflated and low surface brightness lobes, with the E lobe slightly flattened with major axis along PA 70 ^ and the W lobe closer to the host and extended in the N-S direction, almost perpendicular to the major axis of the source. Several compact sources are superimposed on the lobes and it is difficult to state a precise total flux. There is no radio emission detected at the location of our proposed host 2MASX J14273770+3328081. However, it is the brightest galaxy in both the optical and MIR (unWISE) in the entire region of the radio emission.

gmw_00006563.name: J1433+3450
gmw_00006563.overview: J1433+3450 is a classical, slightly bent Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:2207.01648]. It features a faint radio core detected in both BLDF and VLASS, and it exhibits the highest radio luminosity within its sample [arXiv:2207.01648].

sources.summary: The appendix describes J1433+3450 as a classical slightly bent FR II source with a BLDF/VLASS core-host identification and the highest radio luminosity in the sample.

quotes.quote: J1433+3450 is a classical, slightly bent FR II source with a faint core detected in both BLDF and VLASS. While our proposed host coincides with that radio core, it differs from that proposed by which is off the main radio ridge. The hotspots are strong in VLASS, albeit with much shorter trails towards the host than those in BLDF. This source was first noticed by us in and is also seen in . This is the source with the highest radio luminosity in our sample.

gmw_00006564.name: J1434+3428
gmw_00006564.overview: J1434+3428 is a complex, slightly bent Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:2207.01648]. Its host galaxy remains unclear, but it may be associated with a cluster member that is detected as both an X-ray and radio source [arXiv:2207.01648].

sources.summary: The appendix describes J1434+3428 as a complex bent FR II source with uncertain host identification and a possible alternative AGN-like cluster member host.

quotes.quote: J1434+3428 is a complex, slightly bent FR II source with several radio knots along the E lobe. Both lobes feature hotspots in BLDF which are also faintly detected in VLASS, but which are located due N of the symmetry axis of the lobes. The host of this source is still unclear. We have chosen the brighter one, but 19 '' E of it there is WISEA J143430.65+342757.0 with more typical AGN colors, undetected in the optical in DESI DR9, but found to be a member of cluster ISCS J1434.5+3427 with z_ sp =1.240 from Alberts2016 , also detected as X-ray source 2CXO J143430.6+342757, and as FIRST J143430.6+342757 in the radio. Adopting the latter host this source would be only slightly larger and more radio luminous, as well as a candidate restarted source.

gmw_00006565.name: J1434+3328C
gmw_00006565.overview: J1434+3328C is a candidate hybrid morphology radio galaxy hosted by a broadline quasi-stellar object at a redshift of 0.19756 [arXiv:2207.01648]. The host galaxy is situated within a compact triple of galaxies, and the radio source exhibits a brighter northern lobe of FR II type alongside a fainter southern lobe of FR I type [arXiv:2207.01648].

sources.summary: The appendix treats J1434+3328C as a very large diffuse candidate hybrid source with asymmetric lobes, a broadline-QSO host, X-ray detections, and possible projection effects.

quotes.quote: J1434+3328C is a very large diffuse source of oval shape with major axis near PA 160 ^ with a low surface brightness extension from the end of the N lobe towards SW. The N lobe is much brighter and of FR II type, while the S lobe is fainter and of FR I type, thus it is a candidate hybrid morphology source. The innermost source shows a compact radio knot 6 '' S of the host, also seen in VLASS and Coppejans2015 . An additional diffuse source is seen 16 '' from the host with no optical/IR counterpart. The host itself is a broadline QSO at z_ sp =0.19756 (from SDSS) and is apparently located in a compact triple of galaxies.

gmw_00006566.name: J1438+3355
gmw_00006566.overview: J1438+3355 is a giant radio galaxy with a large linear size of 3.8 Mpc [arXiv:2207.01648]. Its outer lobes are detached from the host galaxy, showing short radio trails, a steep spectrum, and high radio luminosity [arXiv:2207.01648].

sources.summary: The appendix identifies J1438+3355 as one of the largest sources, with detached aligned lobes, short radio trails, high radio luminosity, and a steep spectrum.

quotes.quote: J1438+3355: this is the third-largest source in angular size but the one of largest linear size (3.8 Mpc) in our sample. The outer lobes are very detached from the host, but both show the characteristic (though short) trail of radio emission from the far end of the lobes towards the host. The lobe length ratio is only 1.4 (NE/SW lobe) and both lobes are aligned with the host to within 1.5 ^ . The source has a radio luminosity in the upper quartile and a rather steep spectrum ( >1.17 ).

gmw_00006567.name: J1440+3211
gmw_00006567.overview: J1440+3211 is an angularly large Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:2207.01648]. It features detached, parallel-aligned lobes with radio trails, where only the northern lobe contains a detected hotspot [arXiv:2207.01648].

sources.summary: The appendix describes J1440+3211 as an angularly large FR II source with detached aligned lobes, weak or absent core detections, and a VLASS hotspot only in the north lobe.

quotes.quote: J1440+3211 is one of the angularly largest sources in our sample, this is an FR II source with very detached lobes, both of them showing radio trails from their end towards the host, which both lobes being oriented parallel to within less than 1 ^ . The host is barely detected in BLDF, and undetected in VLASS. The N lobe shows a prominent hotspot in VLASS while in the S lobe none is detected.

gmw_00006568.name: 3C006.1
gmw_00006568.overview: 3C006.1 is a classical Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.84 [arXiv:1801.01751]. Although it features long lobes and distinct hot spots, its radio core was not detected in VLA maps [arXiv:1801.01751], and its X-ray emission shows no detectable variability between observations [arXiv:astro-ph/0511606].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C006.1 as a high-redshift classical FR II radio source whose core was not detected, with broad radio coverage but difficult KDA/KDA EXT model fits.

quotes.quote: 3C006.1 is an example of classical FR II-type radio source with long lobes and distinct hot spots. It lies at relatively high redshift (z = 0.84) and was previously studied by Neff et al. (1995). It is worth noting that the radio core of this galaxy was not detected in the VLA maps at 6 cm, and the authors argue that its nucleus may have changed its spatial orientation. This guess is based on radio morphology of the source: a faint inner lobe structure is visible on VLA radio maps at 20 cm.

The radio spectrum coverage for this source extends from 74 MHz up to 21.7 GHz . Such a broad spectrum usually results in reliable KDA EXT or KDA model fits to the observed flux densities. However, in this case the strong bend typical for old FR II-type sources is visible only at extremely high frequencies (starting from 10.7 GHz), and both fitted models are rather poor, especially in comparison with other objects from the sample.

sources.summary: The core was observed in two Chandra exposures, yielding hundreds of counts per spectrum; simultaneous fitting found no need for pileup and no significant variability.

quotes.quote: 3C 6.1 : the source was observed in two separate exposures of 36 ks (Obs ID 3009) and 20 ks (Obs ID 4363), respectively. We extracted the spectrum in the same region (a circle of radius 2.5 pixels) for the two exposures. Observation 3009 contains 1568 net counts in the 0.2-10.0 keV energy band. Observation 4363 contains 758 counts in the same energy band. The two spectra were fitted simultaneously.

X-ray emission is also observed from hotspots mjh04 and from the lobes croston05 . With a mean flux of 0.044 0.008 for Obs ID 3009 and of 0.039 0.014 for Obs ID 4363, no significant variability can be claimed.

gmw_00006569.name: 3C325
gmw_00006569.overview: 3C325 is a red quasar located at a redshift of 1.135, having been reclassified from its original designation as a radio galaxy [arXiv:1801.01751, arXiv:2011.03130]. It exhibits a classical Fanaroff-Riley II morphology with a 124 kpc projected radio jet, asymmetric lobes, and bright hot spots [arXiv:1801.01751, arXiv:2011.03130].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C325 as the sample’s highest-redshift galaxy, with curved spectrum, large bright-lobed FR II structure, and mixed KDA EXT fit quality.

quotes.quote: 3C325 With z= 1.135, 3C325 is the most redshifted galaxy in the sample. It is characterised by a highly curved radio spectrum and a large radio structure with bright lobes. Its classical FR II-type morphology is associated with a parent quasar (Véron-Cetty Véron 2006). Preference of the KDA EXT over KDA solution is not evident: the calculated value is exactly equal to the assumed significance level for the adopted statistical test. Figure 6 shows that this model fits the high-frequency part of the radio spectrum especially well, while departing from the low flux density data points except for the 150 MHz TGSS flux (see also Table 7).

sources.summary: The paper says 3C 325 was reclassified from radio galaxy to red quasar, has a 124 kpc projected radio jet, weak 5 GHz core, VLA hot spots and asymmetric lobes.

quotes.quote: -3C 325 was originally classified as a radio galaxy, and later was reclassified as a red quasar Grimes2005 based on optical spectroscopic data. This quasar has a projected radio jet of 124 kpc and less than 1 core contribution at 5 GHz. Because the jet inclination angle for this object is unknown, we were unable to estimate its de-projected jet length. The VLA images show multiple features including bright hot spots and asymmetrically placed lobes Fernini1997 . The radio data is best fitted with a double power-law which turns down before reaching the submm wavelengths.

gmw_00006570.name: 3C401
gmw_00006570.overview: 3C401 is a small, low-redshift Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy that coincides with a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:1801.01751]. It is a low-excitation radio galaxy characterized by a medium-brightness radio core [arXiv:1801.01751], diffuse hotspots, and a jet detected at high radio frequencies [arXiv:0804.3369].

sources.summary: The paper detects 3C 401 jet emission at 90 GHz, argues its spectrum breaks between centimeter and millimeter wavelengths, and notes no resolved lobe steepening.

quotes.quote: Jet-related emission is clearly detected in three of the newly observed sources, 3C 388, 3C 401, and 3C 438. This is the first time that FRII jets have been detected at these high radio frequencies. The three detected sources are all part of the class of low-excitation radio galaxies with prominent jets and diffuse hotspots, possibly lying in rich environments, identified by Hardcastle \ (1997).

Chiaberge \ (2005) report a detection of the 3C 401 jet in Hubble Space Telescope near-infrared images, implying a flux density of 6.0 Jy at 190 THz, which would correspond to a spectral index between the mm and infrared of 0.74 : thus it seems that the spectrum of the jet breaks at frequencies between 10 and 100 GHz and then remains roughly constant over many decades in frequency. In 3C 401 and 3C 438 the flattest-spectrum regions are the jets (already discussed above) and there is no clear evidence for resolved spectral steepening in the lobes.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C401 as a small low-redshift FR II source coincident with a Seyfert galaxy, with a medium-bright radio core and no statistical preference for KDA EXT.

quotes.quote: 3C401 3C401 is a rather small ( D= 67.5 kpc) and low-redshifted ( z=0.201 ) FR II-type radio source previously studied by Laing et al. (1983). It coincides with a Seyfert galaxy and is characterised by a radio core of medium brightness equal to 28.54 0.03 mJy (Hardcastle et al. 1998) and high axial ratio of the lobes. In this case, there is no statistical preference of the KDA EXT model fit over the KDA model, with the value being almost seven times larger than the level of significance. The spectra resulting from the predicted flux densities S_ MOD (KDA) and S_ MOD (KDA EXT) are listed in Table 8, along with their corresponding goodness of the fit to ^ 2 data, which is evidently lower in the case of the KDA EXT fit.

gmw_00006571.name: HR 10
gmw_00006571.overview: HR 10 is an extremely red object located at a redshift of z = 1.44 [arXiv:astro-ph/0111243]. It is an interacting system [arXiv:astro-ph/9801150] classified as an ultraluminous infrared galaxy with a high star formation rate [arXiv:astro-ph/0111243, arXiv:astro-ph/9801150].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HR 10 as a z=1.44 ultra-luminous infrared ERO with an Arp 220-like SED, very high infrared luminosity, and starburst-versus-AGN power-source ambiguity.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of the extremely red object (ERO), HR 10 ( I-K 6.5 , z =1.44), at 4.9 and 6.1 m (rest-frame) with ISOCAM, the mid-infrared (MIR) camera onboard the Infrared Space Observatory (ISO). HR 10 is the first ERO spectroscopically identified to be associated with an ultra-luminous IR galaxy (ULIG) detected in the radio, MIR and sub-millimeter. The rest-frame spectral energy distribution (SED) of HR 10 is amazingly similar to the one of Arp 220, scaled by a factor 3.8 1.3 . The corresponding 8-1000 m luminosity ( 7 10 ^ 12 h_ 70 ^ 2 L_ ) translates into a star formation rate of about 1200 h_ 70 ^ 2 M_ yr ^ 1 if HR 10 is mostly powered by star formation. We address the key issue of the origin of the powerful luminosity of HR 10, i.e. starburst versus active galactic nucleus (AGN), by using the similarity with its closeby clone, Arp 220.

sources.summary: The paper says object 10 is extremely red, undetected in U,V,R to faint limits, and follow-up infrared spectroscopy showed it was an interacting system with possible H emission and strong star formation.

quotes.quote: Objects 10 and 14 cannot be catalogued this way because they escape detection down to very faint magnitudes ( U>26^m and V>26^m ). They are very good candidates for EROS and that prompted the observations of object 10 doing deep IR imaging and spectroscopy (Graham Dey ), finding --- as expected --- that it could not be an elliptical galaxy but rather an interacting system. A possible H emission at z 1.44 was detected, revealing quite a strong star formation rate.

gmw_00006572.name: PKS 0244-470
gmw_00006572.overview: PKS 0244-470 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar blazar located at a redshift of z = 1.385 [arXiv:2211.00588]. The active galactic nucleus has exhibited both active and quiescent phases, including a 225-day transient gamma-ray quasi-periodic oscillation during its active phase [arXiv:2211.00588].

sources.summary: The paper studies PKS 0244-470 as an FSRQ blazar, finding activity and quiescent phases and a 225-day transient gamma-ray QPO in its AP-1 phase.

quotes.quote: PKS 0244-47 is an FSRQ type blazar with coordinate RA = 02h46m00.00s, DEC = -46d51m18.4s (J2000) located at redshift, z = 1.385 2010ApJ...715..429A . It is identified as flaring ray blazar in 2010 2010ATel.2440....1E and was very active till 2013 (see the ray light curve on https://fermi.gsfc.nasa.gov/ssc/data/access/lat/LightCurveRepository/source.html?source_name=4FGL_J0245.9-4650 Fermi repository ). Since 2013 the source is in a very low state, and few studies have been done. This is the first time we are presenting a QPO study on this source.

Combining this with the source's intrinsic variance, we identified two flux phases for PKS 0244-470: one activity (AP-1) and one quiescent phase (QP-1). For 4C+38.41, we similarly identified four activity (AP-1, AP-2, AP-3, AP-4) and two quiescent (QP-1, QP-2) phases. AP-1 phase of PKS 0244-470 shows QPO of 225 days persisting for 8 cycles ( 4.1 ).

gmw_00006573.name: Mrk 67
gmw_00006573.overview: Mrk 67 is a Wolf-Rayet galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0204301]. Its neutral hydrogen envelope is strongly affected by stellar feedback [arXiv:astro-ph/0204301].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Mrk 67 as an interesting Wolf-Rayet sample galaxy whose H i envelope is strongly affected by stellar feedback and merits detailed extended H i observations.

quotes.quote: We find one interesting sample galaxy, Mrk 67, which is plotted at ( M (H i ), L (H )) = ( 10.0^ 7.4 M_ , 10.0^ 39.9 ) in Figure 1. It is a Wolf-Rayet galaxy (Conti 1991). This galaxy is located near the solid line. The H i envelope is strongly affected by stellar feedback. Thus, if we want to know the precise effect of the low density halo, the detailed observation of the more extended H i envelope of Mrk 67 is important.

gmw_00006574.name: MRG-S0851
gmw_00006574.overview: MRG-S0851 is a massive, compact quiescent galaxy at redshift z = 1.88 that is quintuply imaged via strong gravitational lensing [arXiv:2103.03255]. Although it has a low star formation rate, the galaxy shows evidence of a centrally concentrated rejuvenation event within the last 100 million years [arXiv:2103.03255].

sources.summary: The paper studies MRG-S0851 as a lensed compact quiescent galaxy at z=1.88, constraining its molecular gas fraction and interpreting rapid depletion after recent rejuvenated star formation.

quotes.quote: MRG-S0851 appears to be a prototypical massive compact quiescent galaxy, but has evidence that it experienced a centrally concentrated rejuvenation event in the last 100 Myr (see mob ). This galaxy is undetected in the AzTEC image but we calculate an upper limit on the millimeter flux and use this to estimate the H _2 mass limit via an empirically calibrated relation that assumes a constant molecular gas-to-dust ratio of 150. We constrain the 3 upper limit of the H _2 fraction from the dust continuum in MRG-S0851 to be M_ H_2 /M_ 6.8 .

black Galaxy MRG-S0851 is a red galaxy at z =1.88 quintuply imaged via strong gravitational lensing black by the cluster SDSS J0851 + 3331 identified in the Sloan Giant Arc Survey (Figure fig:hstcolor ; see also sharon20 ). As a target in the REsolving QUIEscent Magnified (REQUIEM) galaxy survey, mo20 present the analysis of the stellar population gradients of our main target with surprising evidence supporting a rejuvenation phase in the last 100 Myr mob . MRG-S0851 is otherwise a prototypical z 2 quiescent galaxy, with an intrinsic stellar mass of M_* /M _ = 11.02 0.04, a low SFR of 6 1M _ /yr, sSFR log(SFR/M _ ) = -10.32 _ 0.05 ^ +0.07 yr ^ 1 , a S e rsic index of n=2.35 0.05 , black and a compact circularized effective radius of 1.7 ^ +0.3 _ 0.1 kpc.

MRG-S0851 has a rapid depletion time with an upper limit of 0.6 Gyr, about 10 times faster than similar quiescent galaxies at z =0. This implies that gas stabilization from collapse, a mechanism which has been invoked by Suess17 to explain high gas fractions, does not play a key role, at least for MRG-S0851.

gmw_00006575.name: UGC 2080
gmw_00006575.overview: UGC 2080 is classified as a Type 2 irregular dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1103.4928]. It exhibits globally offset iso-velocity contours and a high kinematic perturbation parameter, reflecting a constant, large difference between its receding and approaching sides [arXiv:1103.4928].

sources.summary: The paper presents UGC 2080 as a Type 2 irregular dwarf with globally offset iso-velocity contours and a high kinematic perturbation parameter.

quotes.quote: As an example we show the irregular dwarf galaxy UGC 2080 (Fig. fig_examples , second row). There are no local asymmetries in the velocity field, but a global offset of the iso-velocity contours.
For UGC 2080, _ kin is very high, which reflects very nicely the constant, large difference between receding and approaching side.

gmw_00006576.name: UGC 9211
gmw_00006576.overview: UGC 9211 is classified as a Type 3 irregular dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1103.4928]. Its receding-side rotation velocity declines significantly at large radii, which is likely caused by gas attached to the northwestern part of the galaxy [arXiv:1103.4928].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 9211 as a Type 3 irregular dwarf whose receding-side rotation declines at large radius, likely because gas is attached northwest of the galaxy.

quotes.quote: A good example is the irregular dwarf galaxy UGC 9211 (Fig. fig_examples , third row). Whereas the rotation velocities extracted from the approaching side form a typical rotation curve with a slow rise in the inner parts and a plateau in the outer parts, the rotation velocities extracted from the receding side start to decline significantly from a radius of 6 R_ 25 on. The velocity field shows gas which seems to be attached to the north-western part of the galaxy and probably causes the unexpected shape of the rotation curve of the receding side.
In UGC 9211, _ kin is clearly below the mean. However, the parameter does not account for the large discrepancies in the very outer parts of the galaxy.

gmw_00006577.name: UGC 1249
gmw_00006577.overview: UGC 1249 is an irregular companion galaxy [arXiv:1012.0816] that is interacting with the nearby spiral galaxy NGC 672 [arXiv:1012.0816, arXiv:0808.1789]. It is also classified as a Type 2 galaxy due to a constant offset in its rotation curves, likely caused by interactions with its more massive northern companion, UGC 1256 [arXiv:1103.4928].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 1727 as NGC 672’s irregular companion, detected in continuum and kinematically connected by H I; its main body rotates regularly while the pair shows disturbed outskirts.

quotes.quote: NGC 672 is a moderately inclined spiral which has a neighboring irregular galaxy, IC 1727. In Figure figure:n0672 , NGC 672 is the brighter galaxy centered in the frames, and IC 1727 is located to its southwest. At the adopted distance listed in Table table:sample (7.6 Mpc), the linear projected separation between the two galaxies is 16.3 kpc.

Both galaxies are clearly detected in continuum emission. The \ distribution shown in Figure figure:n0672 reveals clearly the connection between the two galaxies. Each of the two galaxies appears to be relatively undisturbed within their optical extent, but the outskirts and interface region are more complicated.
The main body of IC 1727 appears to show well ordered rotation.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 1727 as a disturbed late-type member of the NGC 672 pair, with blue condensations, many HII regions, and two modeled star-formation bursts.

quotes.quote: IC 1727 was classified by the same authors as IrrI and SBm. It shows a disturbed spiral shape with a bright central ridge of blue condensations, possibly an incipient bar.

34 HII regions were reported in NGC 672 and 28 in IC 1727 (e.g, Hodge 1982, Hodge Kennicutt 1983). Both NGC 672 and IC 1727 have colours typical of field galaxies. These fitted the models reasonably well ( ^ 2 < 3 ), indicating two SF bursts at similar times for both galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper classifies UGC 1249 as a strongly affected Type 2 galaxy with a constant rotation-curve offset and an interacting, more massive companion.

quotes.quote: UGC 1249 shows a constant offset of 50 \ between the rotation curves of receding and approaching sides and is therefore classified as Type 2 galaxy. The velocity field reveals a close companion in the north, UGC 1256, which is at least twice as massive as UGC 1249. Both galaxies seem to interact with each other. While UGC 1249 is strongly affected by this process, UGC 1256 appears to be not lopsided at all (therefore classified as Type 1).

gmw_00006578.name: UGC 1317
gmw_00006578.overview: UGC 1317 is a member of a five-galaxy group that also includes UGC 1280, UGC 1286, UGC 1305, and UGC 1310 [arXiv:1103.4928]. The galaxy is classified as Type 2 due to a persistent side-to-side offset in its rotation curve, which declines in its outer regions [arXiv:1103.4928].

sources.summary: The paper places UGC 1317 in a five-galaxy group and classifies it as Type 2 because its rotation curve declines outward with a persistent side-to-side offset.

quotes.quote: UGC 1317 is in a group with four other galaxies (UGC 1280, UGC 1286, UGC 1305, and UGC 1310). It shows a declining rotation curve in the outer parts. Receding and approaching sides are offset by about 50 \ at all radii, which classifies this galaxy as Type 2.

gmw_00006579.name: UGC 2855
gmw_00006579.overview: UGC 2855 is a large SBc barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9903409] that features a molecular gas-filled bar in a pre-starburst stage [arXiv:astro-ph/9903409]. The galaxy has a less massive companion, UGC 2866 [arXiv:1103.4928], and exhibits a northwestern tail alongside a rotation-curve asymmetry in its outer regions [arXiv:1103.4928].

sources.summary: The paper presents UGC 2855 as an SBc barred spiral with an extensive molecular gas bar, strong molecular emission, and a likely young pre-starburst bar.

quotes.quote: UGC 2855 is a large spiral galaxy, almost the same size as the Milky Way, classified as SBc (RC3, de Vaucouleurs et al. \ 1991).

As it is typical for barred spirals, its main spiral arms start at the ends of the bar, and strong molecular emission can be traced in both the bar and the arms. The distribution of ^ 12 CO We present the total integrated intensity map, the velocity field and the map of the velocity dispersion from our interferometric ^ 12 CO observations in Fig. 1. From Fig. 1 a, it is clear that the bar in UGC 2855 is filled with molecular gas over its entire length.

We suggest that the bar of UGC 2855 is a young object, possibly has no ILR, and that the mass concentration toward the center of the galaxy is just beginning. In contrast, the companion UGC 2866 experiences a strong starburst. We compare the properties of the gas in the bar of UGC 2855 to those we find in NGC 7479. Despite superficial parallels, the two bars are very dissimilar objects in terms of ^ 12 CO/ ^ 13 CO line ratio, velocity field and H activity. NGC 7479 exhibits starburst characteristics, while we argue that UGC 2855 is in a pre-burst stage.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 2855 as having a less massive companion, a northwest tail, southern gas, and a Type 3 outer rotation-curve asymmetry exceeding 50 km/s.

quotes.quote: UGC 2855 has a close companion, UGC 2866, which is three times less massive. UGC 2855 shows a tail in the north-western part and additional gas attached to the southern edge. Receding and approaching sides are in good agreement in the inner parts. However, in the outer parts, the approaching rotation curve shows a decline, whereas the receding one slowly increases (Type 3). The difference in velocity is more than 50 .

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 2855 as having high nuclear nitrogen emission, lower disk ratios, an arm arc of emission, and central extended emission associated with ENER-like behavior.

quotes.quote: UGC 2855 Figure fig:u2141_u2210_u2855 (right) shows that there is a high ratio of 6583 to \ in the nucleus, but this ratio falls significantly in the disk. There is an arc of 6583 emission, however, located at the eastern end of one of the spiral arms. The asymptotic value of 6583/ \ is 0.18. /( \ + total) is, therefore, 0.80 for the whole galaxy.

gmw_00006580.name: UGC 4173
gmw_00006580.overview: UGC 4173 is a slowly rotating galaxy featuring a faint optical disk surrounding a central bar-like structure [arXiv:0901.4222, arXiv:1103.4928]. Its neutral hydrogen gas extends out to nearly ten optical radii [arXiv:1103.4928] and exhibits a smooth southeastern asymmetry with lopsidedness that increases with radius [arXiv:1103.4929].

sources.summary: The paper uses UGC 4173 as an example of a galaxy whose H I lopsidedness rises smoothly with radius, with a smooth distribution and southeastern asymmetry.

quotes.quote: To get a clearer picture, we selected two galaxies and compared their global profile, the \ morphology, and the distributions of A_1 and _1 with radius. UGC 4173 (see Fig.  figexamples , upper row) represents a type of galaxy where we found the expected behaviour of increasing lopsidedness with radius. As can be seen, the \ distribution is quite smooth with a clear asymmetry in the south-east.

This is exactly confirmed for galaxies like UGC 4173 or UGC 4278 which show a smooth radial increase in A_1 (Fig.  figlop_2 , this paper) and both have solid body rotation in the corresponding radial region as seen in Fig. A1, Paper I.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4173 as a faint disk around a central bar-like structure, but says the bar is not clearly reflected in the velocity field.

quotes.quote: 4173 has a faint optical disk surrounding a central bar-like structure. The kinematical major axis is aligned with the faint disk. The bar is not clearly reflected in the velocity field.

sources.summary: The paper reports that MOND overpredicts UGC 4173's rotation velocities; its optical bar and faint disk make inclination uncertain, and a lower inclination would improve MOND and Tully-Fisher consistency.

quotes.quote: UGC 4173. -- The MOND curve based on the \ alone already predicts significantly higher rotation velocities than are observed. This galaxy has an optical bar, with a faint surrounding disk, making the inclination difficult to determine. An inclination of 25 ^ to 30 ^ is consistent with the \ morphology, and would make this galaxy consistent with MOND, and also bring it closer to the Tully-Fisher relation.

sources.summary: The paper highlights UGC 4173 as a slowly rotating Type 2 galaxy with modest side-to-side differences but neutral gas detected to nearly ten optical radii.

quotes.quote: UGC 4173 is very slowly rotating with only 36 \ (there is only one more galaxy, UGC 2034, in our sample which shows an even lower rotation velocity of about 24 ). Receding and approaching sides differ by about 10 \ across the whole radial range (Type 2). The most remarkable thing about this galaxy is the extent to which we detect the neutral gas, which is out to almost 10 R_ 25 .

gmw_00006581.name: UGC 4458
gmw_00006581.overview: UGC 4458 is a galaxy with a low-mass companion that is approximately six times less massive [arXiv:1103.4928]. The galaxy appears to be interacting with this companion, displaying very large rotation-curve offsets at all radii and unusually high rotation velocities [arXiv:1103.4928].

sources.summary: The paper says UGC 4458 has a low-mass close companion, very large all-radius rotation-curve offsets, unusually high velocities, and apparent interaction with that companion.

quotes.quote: UGC 4458 also reveals a close companion, which is quite mass-poor (about six times less massive than UGC 4458). The offset between receding and approaching sides is visible at all radii (Type 2) and very large with 100 to 150 . In fact, the rotation velocities are unusually high with values of about 550 \ (approaching side) and 350 \ (receding side) at 0.75 R_ 25 , decreasing at larger radii to 400 and 300 \ respectively. However, note that the inclination is very low. From the velocity field it seems that UGC 4458 interacts with its small companion.

gmw_00006582.name: F579-VI
gmw_00006582.overview: F579-VI is a bulgeless, low surface brightness disk galaxy [arXiv:1707.08085]. It is characterized by a low central neutral hydrogen surface density and a stable galactic disk [arXiv:1707.08085].

sources.summary: The paper treats F579-VI as one of five bulgeless LSB sample galaxies with low surface brightness, similar disc scales and masses, low central H I density, and stable discs.

quotes.quote: Our LSB sample consist of F563-1, F568-1, F568-3, F568-VI and F579-VI. They have a B-band central surface surface brightness _B (0) ranging between 23-24 mg arcsec ^ 2 , spanning exponential disc scalelength R_D between 2.8 - 5.3 kpc and comparable dynamical masses as given by the asymptotic rotational velocity V _ max 105 - 118 kms ^ 1 , except for F568-1 which has a slightly higher V _ max 142 kms ^ 1 . All our galaxies are bulgeless. Our sample LSBs have a central HI surface density a few M _ pc ^ 2 .

We find that the median value of Q _ RW ^ min , the minimum of Q _ RW over R , lies between 2.6 and 3.1 for our sample LSBs, which is higher than the median value of 1.8 0.3 for Q _ RW ^ min for a sample of high surface brightness galaxies (HSBs) as obtained in earlier studies.

gmw_00006583.name: J1243+4646
gmw_00006583.overview: J1243+4646 is a compact star-forming galaxy at low redshift [arXiv:2202.07768]. It is a Lyman alpha emitter that exhibits the highest known low-redshift Lyman continuum escape fraction [arXiv:2202.07768].

sources.summary: The paper presents J1243+4646 as a compact star-forming LyC-emitting galaxy with the highest known low-redshift LyC escape fraction and unusually strong UV emission-line properties.

quotes.quote: Using the Space Telescope Imaging Spectrograph, we have obtained ultraviolet (UV) spectra from 1200 to 2000 \ of known Lyman continuum (LyC) emitting galaxies at low redshift ( z 0.3-0.4 ) with varying absolute LyC escape fractions ( 0.01 - 0.72 ). Our observations include in particular the galaxy J1243+4646, which has the highest known LyC escape fraction at low redshift. While all galaxies are known Lyman alpha emitters, we consistently detect an inventory of additional emission lines, including , , , and , whose origin is presumably essentially nebular.

Here, we present the first results of an HST program to observe the UV emission lines of known LyC emitters, sampling a range of LyC escape fractions from very low ( = 1.4 ) to the highest escape currently known ( = 72 ), which was observed in the compact star-forming galaxy J1243+4646 by .

We report the detection above 4 of \ emission in six out of eight galaxies, with the EWs in two galaxies ( J1243+4646 and J1248+4259 ), EW ( )=12-15 , \ exceeding the previously reported maximum in low- z galaxies J104457 ; Berg2019Intense-C-IV-an .

gmw_00006584.name: J1154+2443
gmw_00006584.overview: J1154+2443 is a compact starburst galaxy classified as a Lyman continuum emitter [arXiv:2202.07768]. It is a highly leaky galaxy with a high Lyman continuum escape fraction [arXiv:2009.09882] and optical line ratios that are characteristic of strong leakers [arXiv:2202.07768].

sources.summary: The paper includes J1154+2443 among observed LyC emitters, adds unavailable STIS observations that improve its spectrum, and notes optical line ratios similar to values expected for the strongest leakers.

quotes.quote: The galaxies are J1243+4646 , J1154+2443 , J1152+3400 , J1442-0209 , J0925+1403 , J1011+1947 , J0901+2119 , and J1248+4259 . . The sources span a broad range of LyC escape fractions ( 1.4-72 ), metallicities in the range = 7.64 - 8.16 with a median of 7.92, and / \ from 5 to 27.1. The observations were taken with the Space Telescope Imaging Spectrograph (STIS) NUV-MAMA using the grating G230L with the central wavelength 2376 \ and the slit 52 0 5, resulting in a spectral resolution of R 700 for the targeted compact galaxies. The data were reduced with the CALSTIS v3.4.2 pipeline and including data from additional observations of J1154+2443 that were not available to , which improved the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) to 7 per pixel for this galaxy.

This hardness translates to optical line ratios of I( )/I( ) 1.74 Q_ He+ /Q_ H 0.016 - 0.07 , similar to the observations of J1154+2443 and J1248+4259 , which are I(4686)/I( ) 0.02-0.03 Guseva2020Properties-of-f , and comparable to the line intensities typically observed in galaxies at comparable metallicity at 8.0 ; see, e.g., Schaerer2019X-ray-binaries- .

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1154+2443 as the most leaky galaxy in its sample, with observed escape fraction broadly consistent with two-zone density-bounded-channel model predictions.

quotes.quote: For the five leaking galaxies with \ measurements, the observed escape fractions seem consistent with predictions from the DBC scenario, even for the most leaky galaxy J1154+2443 (red circle, =46 ). Indeed, we note that this galaxy stands on the left-hand side of the median line, as expected for the highest escape fractions produced by the DBC scenario; it falls between two model points with escape fractions of 30--40 , which is roughly consistent with the observed value.
The three strongest leakers (J1256+4509, J1154+2443 and J1243+4646), which have 30 all exhibit a large deficiency in \ of at least 0.2 dex from the ridge line (> 1 ).

gmw_00006585.name: J1248+4259
gmw_00006585.overview: J1248+4259 is a low-redshift emission-line galaxy [arXiv:2202.07768] that exhibits exceptionally strong Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:2009.09882, arXiv:2202.07768]. It displays a low observed Lyman continuum escape fraction of only a few percent [arXiv:2009.09882, arXiv:2202.07768], which may be underestimated due to non-isotropic emission [arXiv:2009.09882, arXiv:2202.07768].

sources.summary: The paper treats J1248+4259 as an unusual low-redshift LyC emitter, with very strong emission, a high carbon-line ratio, a double-peaked line profile, and hints of higher true escape fraction.

quotes.quote: Furthermore, J1248+4259 stands out as the low- z galaxy ( z 0.4 ) with the highest \ EW (EW ( )=15.14 2.14 ); however, it shows a low escape fraction ( = 0.014 0.004 ). Again, the observed EWs are also in fair agreement with the predictions from photoionization models Nakajima2018The-VIMOS-Ultra,Ravindranath2020The-Semiforbidd .

If generally applicable, our postulate implies that the galaxy J1248+4259 reported by should be a strong LyC emitter; that is, it should \ have a true LyC escape fraction of at least 5 times that measured from the COS spectrum, which could be possible \ if, for example, the emission of LyC photons is not isotropic. There are indeed other indications that J1248+4259 could be a strong leaker: First, it shows a very strong \ emission with an EW of EW ( )=256 5.2 , comparable to strong leakers. Second, the \ line profile is clearly double-peaked, with a fairly low separation of the two peaks ( v_ sep = 283.8 15.9 ), which would yield = 0.13 if the mean relation between \ and v_ sep from was applied. Finally, J1248+4259 is also somewhat of an ``outlier'' in the scattered relation between O32 = / \ and , showing a lower-than-average escape fraction for its high O32=11.8 see Izotov2021Lyman-continuum .

This hardness translates to optical line ratios of I( )/I( ) 1.74 Q_ He+ /Q_ H 0.016 - 0.07 , similar to the observations of J1154+2443 and J1248+4259 , which are I(4686)/I( ) 0.02-0.03 Guseva2020Properties-of-f , and comparable to the line intensities typically observed in galaxies at comparable metallicity at 8.0 ; see, e.g., Schaerer2019X-ray-binaries- .

sources.summary: The paper treats J1248+4259 as a deficient LyC-leaking galaxy with only a few percent escape fraction and unusually strong emission despite high H I covering fraction.

quotes.quote: However, we note that three galaxies in our sample with a large deficit in \ ( 0.3) have intermediate (J1011+1947, f_ esc =11,4 ) to low escape fractions (J1248+4259, J1333+6246, labeled on the plot).

The three strongest leakers (J1256+4509, J1154+2443 and J1243+4646), which have 30 all exhibit a large deficiency in \ of at least 0.2 dex from the ridge line (> 1 ). J1011+1947, with an intermediate escape fraction of 11.4 also shows a large \ deficit. However, the most deficient galaxy in our sample (J1248+4259) and J1333+6246, which is undetected in , have escape fractions of only a few percent. This could mean that the observed \ for these galaxies is underestimated, for example due to the fact that LyC radiation escapes only along other, unobserved lines of sight.

Interestingly, in the analysis of the UV absorption lines of more than 30 low- z galaxies from , J1248+4259 stands out as an outlier with an exceptionally strong \ emission (high equivalent width) despite showing a large covering fraction in the H i absorption lines.

gmw_00006586.name: 2MASX 17171926+4226571
gmw_00006586.overview: 2MASX 17171926+4226571 is the brightest cluster galaxy in the ZwCl 8193 cluster [arXiv:1606.04067]. It is located near the radio source B3 1715+425, though Very Long Baseline Array observations show no radio emission brighter than 1 mJy beam at the galaxy's position [arXiv:1606.04067].

sources.summary: The paper describes 2MASX 17171926+4226571 as the BCG in ZwCl 8193, separating it from the offset radio galaxy and arguing the BCG contributes little radio flux.

quotes.quote: The BCG in ZwCl 8193 is the 2MASS galaxy 2MASX 17171926+4226571 at PSC position J2000 = 17^ h 17^ m 19 245 , = +42^ 26' 57 48 . Its S_ 1.4 GHz = 133.5 4.0 mJy NVSS counterpart NVSS J171719+422659 NVSS J171719 +422659 at J2000 = 17^ h 17^ m 19 18 0 04 , = +42^ 26' 59 6 0 6 is > 2'' north of the BCG. This radio source was first detected at S = 0.2 Jy in the 408 MHz Bologna B3 survey fic85 and named B3 1715+425 (equinox B1950 IAU format name).

Our VLBA images reveal no radio emission brighter than 1 mJy beam at the position of the BCG. FIRST bec95 ( 5 4 FWHM resolution) does not resolve B3 1715+425, and the FIRST flux density S 132.5 mJy equals the NVSS ( 45'' FWHM resolution) flux density S = 133.5 4.0 mJy , so B3 1715+425 has very little or no extended radio emission on angular scales between 5 4 and 45'' . The 2 8 angular separation between B3 1715+425 and the BCG is smaller than the FIRST beam, so the BCG might be a fairly compact radio source, smaller than the FIRST beam but large enough to be resolved out by the VLBA.

gmw_00006587.name: SDSS J153037.80+265856.8/LEDA 1794348
gmw_00006587.overview: SDSS J153037.80+265856.8/LEDA 1794348 is a lenticular host galaxy located at a redshift of 0.0325 [arXiv:2304.12661]. Prior to hosting the optical flare AT2020vwl, the galaxy exhibited a quiescent spectrum with no signs of active star formation or strong active galactic nucleus emission [arXiv:2304.12661].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the host of AT2020vwl as a lenticular, quiescent galaxy lacking strong AGN, star-formation, and pre-flare radio signatures, so the radio emission is attributed to the transient.

quotes.quote: AT2020vwl (also Gaia20etp, ZTF20achpcvt) was first discovered by the Gaia Spacecraft on 2020 October 10 as an optical flare of 1 mag above the quiescent galaxy flux, localised to the centre of the galaxy SDSS J153037.80+265856.8/LEDA 1794348 J2000 RA, Dec 15:30:37.800, +26:58:56.89, Gaia_ATel . The event was also observed by the Zwicky-Transient Facility (ZTF) on 2020 October 8, but was not reported until 2020 December 20 ZTF_ATel,Yao2023 . A follow-up observation with the SED Machine integral field unit Spectrograph at the Palomar 60-inch on 2020 December 21 showed a spectrum with a steep blue optical continuum and strong, broad H, HeII, and Balmer lines ZTF_ATel at a redshift of 0.0325, which corresponds to a luminosity distance, D_L = 147 Mpc, and an angular-size distance D_A of 138 Mpc.

The host galaxy of AT2020vwl, SDSS J153037.80+265856.8/LEDA 1794348, appears to be lenticular, with little evidence of spiral structure (Figure fig:host_image ). An SDSS Sloan Digital Sky Survey York2000 spectrum Strauss2002 of the host galaxy taken 13.5 yr prior to the TDE on MJD 54180 shows a quiescent galaxy spectrum with no sign of strong AGN emission lines (e.g. [ OIII ] or [ NII ] ), Figure fig:sdss_spec ). There is no evidence for H emission in the spectrum, which would be indicative of shocked or excited gas due to either AGN activity or active star formation in the galaxy.

gmw_00006588.name: SDSS J1619
gmw_00006588.overview: SDSS J1619 is an active galactic nucleus and emission line galaxy [arXiv:2310.13963]. It is identified as a potential candidate for hosting a recoiling supermassive black hole due to the similar velocity shifts and line widths of its blue-shifted broad H and [O III] components [arXiv:2310.13963].

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSS J1619 as a recoiling-SMBH candidate whose blue-shifted broad H and [O III] components have similar widths and velocity shifts.

quotes.quote: In this Letter, we report a potential candidate of recoiling supermassive black hole (rSMBH) in SDSS J1619 based on similar velocity shifts and line widths of the blue-shifted broad components in H and [ O iii ] doublet. The measured line width ratio between blue-shifted broad H and broad [ O iii ] line is 1.06, if compared with common values around 5.12 for normal Type-1 AGN, indicating different properties of the blue-shifted broad components in SDSS J1619 from those of normal QSOs. The virial BH mass M_ BHr derived from the broad H is consistent with the mass expected from the relation.

The similar velocity shifts and line widths of the blue-shifted broad components in H and [ O iii ] and the virial BH mass derived from the H broad line emissions that is consistent with the mass expected from the relation, can be explained by a rSMBH scenario. Besides the rSMBH scenario, either the similar line widths of the blue-shifted broad components in H and in [ O iii ] or the consistency between the virial BH mass and the mass expected from the relation cannot be explained by the other proposed models in SDSS J1619. active galactic nuclei -- emission line galaxies -- supermassive black holes -- quasars

gmw_00006589.name: WGA J0110 1647
gmw_00006589.overview: WGA J0110 1647 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:0712.1689]. Its X-ray spectrum is well fit by a single power-law with Galactic absorption, though data suggest a broken power-law is a better fit with a break at relatively high energies [arXiv:0712.1689].

sources.summary: The paper fits the X-ray spectrum of WGA J0110 1647, comparing single and broken power-law models and noting the spectral index, break, or soft-excess behavior.

quotes.quote: WGA J0110 1647. The EPIC data are well fit by a single power-law with Galactic absorption and a spectral index of 2 . The data suggest that a broken power-law is a better fit ( F test >99.9 ) than a single power-law. But since the break is at relatively high energies ( E 7 keV), the hard power-law cannot be constrained by the present data.

gmw_00006590.name: WGA J0304 + 0002
gmw_00006590.overview: WGA J0304 + 0002 is a radio-loud quasar classified as a broad-line object [arXiv:0712.1689, arXiv:astro-ph/0509718]. The source exhibits two well-defined radio lobes on each side of its nucleus and displays a soft excess in its X-ray spectrum [arXiv:0712.1689, arXiv:astro-ph/0509718].

sources.summary: The paper fits the X-ray spectrum of WGA J0304 + 0002, comparing single and broken power-law models and noting the spectral index, break, or soft-excess behavior.

quotes.quote: WGA J0304 + 0002. The EPIC data are well fit by a single power-law with Galactic absorption and a spectral index of 1.9 . There is a hint in the data of a soft excess; the fit is marginally improved ( F test 98 ) assuming an absorption lower than Galactic. The fit is also marginally improved ( F test 99 ) if we assume a broken power-law instead of a single power-law.

sources.summary: WGA J0304 + 0002 is classified as a quasar/broad-line object with extended two-lobed radio morphology and a steeper ATCA spectral index than previously listed.

quotes.quote: WGA J0304 + 0002. Optical spectra of this source were published by Per98 and Cac00 who classified it as a quasar and a broad-line object, respectively. This source is also part of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Schn03 . The source appears extended on both the A and C array images (Fig. 1(p) and (q)) with two well-defined lobes on each side of the nucleus and some fuzzy extended emission on larger scales off one of the lobes. Our ATCA snapshot observations of this source give a steeper spectral index of _ =0.71 (between 4.8 and 8.6 GHz) than the one listed in Table general ( _ =0.40 ).

gmw_00006591.name: WGA J0447 0322
gmw_00006591.overview: WGA J0447 0322 is a radio-loud X-ray quasar [arXiv:0712.1689]. Its EPIC X-ray data are well fit by a broken power-law model with Galactic absorption, which improves upon a single power-law fit with an F-test value greater than 99.9 percent [arXiv:0712.1689].

sources.summary: The paper fits the X-ray spectrum of WGA J0447 0322, comparing single and broken power-law models and noting the spectral index, break, or soft-excess behavior.

quotes.quote: WGA J0447 0322. The EPIC data are well fit by a broken power-law with Galactic absorption. This fit represents a significant improvement relative to a fit by a single power-law ( F test >99.9 ).

gmw_00006592.name: RGB J0112 + 3818
gmw_00006592.overview: RGB J0112 + 3818 is a radio-loud X-ray quasar [arXiv:0712.1689]. Its Chandra ACIS X-ray data are well fit by a broken power-law model with Galactic absorption [arXiv:0712.1689].

sources.summary: The paper fits the X-ray spectrum of RGB J0112 + 3818, comparing single and broken power-law models and noting the spectral index, break, or soft-excess behavior.

quotes.quote: RGB J0112 + 3818. The ACIS data are well fit by a broken power-law with Galactic absorption. This fit represents a significant improvement relative to a fit by a single power-law ( F test >99.9 ).

gmw_00006593.name: RGB J2229 + 3057
gmw_00006593.overview: RGB J2229 + 3057 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:0712.1689, arXiv:astro-ph/0509718]. Radio observations reveal extended emission with two well-defined lobes on each side of the nucleus, including a hot spot in the closest lobe [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718].

sources.summary: The paper fits the X-ray spectrum of RGB J2229 + 3057, comparing single and broken power-law models and noting the spectral index, break, or soft-excess behavior.
