Column (1) lists the source name, column (2) gives the redshift of the source, column (3) shows the total number of pulse height analysis (PHA) bins used in the spectrum, column (4) gives the \ of the fit, column (5) gives the reduced , equal to \ divided by the number of degrees of freedom in the fit, column (6) gives the \ in units of 10 ^ 24 , with uncertainties, column (7) gives the photon-index, column (8) gives , determined by the torus model in degrees or the iron abundance with respect to solar hydrogen abundance from the sphere model, column (9) gives the logarithm of the observed 3-79 keV \ flux in \ and column (10) gives the logarithm of the intrinsic (deabsorbed) rest-frame 2-10 keV luminosity in . +u indicates that a parameter has reached the upper limit when estimating the uncertainty. l indicates that the lower limit has been reached. H Column (1) gives the source name, column (2) gives the power-law index of the secondary power-law, column (3) gives the normalization of the secondary power-law, column (4) gives the temperature of the apec model in keV, column (5) gives the normalization of the apec model, column (6) gives the energy of the Gaussian line in keV and column (7) gives the normalisation of the Gaussian line. The best-fit unfolded spectra fit with the torus model are presented in Figure fig_spec . This illustrates the variety of spectral shapes seen above 10 keV for these Compton-thick sources, as well as the range in signal-to-noise in the sample. The data-to-model ratios are presented in Figure fig_ratios which indicate how well the torus model fits the data. The worst reduced \ from the torus fits result from the spectrum of NGC 1068 (1.72), where there is significant curvature in the data-to-model ratio, implying that the torus model is not a good description of the data. This was also the conclusion from the detailed analysis of this source in , which utilizes data from a large array of telescopes and several model combinations. They find that the data require a complex combination of models to reduce this curvature and that a monolithic torus structure is not likely. First in our investigation into what drives the covering factor in AGN, we examine how the measurement of \ in our analysis relates to the other measurements made with the torus model, namely \ and , by plotting these quantities against each other along with their uncertainties (Figure fig_specpar ). No uncertainties can be derived for the =0 \ values assigned to NGC 3079 and NGC 4945 as these have been determined from the sphere model which has a fixed opening angle of zero. There is no clear relationship between these quantities. In our analysis, we fix the torus inclination angle to an edge-on position of 87 . This is primarily motivated by the observation that the inclination angle does not have a large effect on the observed spectrum above 10 keV and thus allows us to reduce the number of free parameters in the fit. Furthermore an edge-on inclination angle allows the full range of opening angles to be explored. Fixing the inclination angle also avoids the scenario where the inclination angle approaches the opening angle, which produces a partially covered solution due to the angular binning in the torus model. We investigate whether allowing the inclination angle to be free allows it to be constrained. This is not the case, however Circinus is an exception, as found by , thus we have allowed the inclination angle to be free for this source. The \ band is also well suited to studying the high-energy cut-off in the X-ray spectra of AGN, and has been used to constrain this in a number of bright unobscured sources e.g. SWIFT J2127.4+5654, IC 4329A, 3C 382, MCG-5-23-16, Balokovi et al. submitted, respectively marinucci14,brenneman14, ballantyne14 . However, the torus model does not include this parameter and thus we have neglected its effect in this work. The potential effect of neglecting the high-energy cut-off here is to over-estimate the Compton hump, which is produced by the down-scattering of high-energy photons. As the covering factor roughly correlates with the strength of the Compton hump, then over-estimating the Compton hump will lead to a systematic under-estimation of the covering factor. We expect this effect to be very small as the lowest high-energy cut-off energy detected so far by \ is at 108 keV in SWIFT J2127.4+5654 marinucci14 , which is far above that of the Compton hump ( 30 keV). For photons originating around a cut-off of this energy to affect the Compton hump, at least 10 scatterings would be required. To understand this effect fully, the torus model should be improved to include the high-energy cut-off. For this study, we have a heterogeneous sample in terms of X-ray spectral coverage and most of our sample have accompanying soft X-ray data to constrain the spectral components below 10 keV. However, for four sources, we use NuSTAR data alone. No soft X-ray data exist for 2MFGC 2280, meanwhile for NGC 1068, NGC 4945 and Circinus, the NuSTAR data are high signal-to-noise enough that they can constrain the torus parameters alone. We test this assumption for all three sources by adding in an \ observation to model the soft X-rays below 3 keV. We find an additional apec model is required to fit these data in addition to the torus and power-law model already in place. Fitting the data simultaneously with the soft component constrained by \ produces only small changes in , and within measurement uncertainties. We conclude that the exclusion of the soft X-ray data does not bias our results significantly in these sources. When fitting \ and \ data together, we have kept the spectral parameters fixed between the two data sets. Since the observations were not made simultaneously, spectral variability could be missed, with the exception of the intrinsic luminosities, which is accounted for by the cross-normalisation between the two observatories being a free parameter. We investigated spectral variability in the five sources where we use both \ and \ data together by allowing the \ parameter to vary between data sets. We find that the \ parameter is consistent within the uncertainties for all of these except NGC 424. For this source, \ measures =4.6^ +1.3 _ 0.7 10^ 24 \ and \ measures =2.1^ +0.6 _ 0.3 10^ 24 . Allowing for the \ to vary like this does not however change our result on . Lastly we investigate how further spectral components added to the fit may affect our results. Specifically, we investigate the addition of the semi-infinite slab reflection model pexrav in order to represent a contribution to reflection from the accretion disc. We use the pure reflection component of this model and fix to that of the torus model, set the high-energy cut-off to maximum ( 1 10^ 6 eV), the adundances to solar and the cosine of the inclination angle to 0.45. The normalization is free. In four sources, NGC 1068, 2MFGC2280, NGC 1320, NGC 1386 and IC 2560, the normalization of the pexrav component falls to zero in the fit. For NGC 3079 and NGC 4945, the addition of the pexrav component has no effect on , whereas for Circinus, the change is 5 . For NGC 424 the addition of pexrav to the fit causes a shift in \ of 25 . While the change in \ is large for NGC 424, when considering the sample as a whole we conclude that the addition of this components does not appear to introduce a systematic effect in the determination of . We note that the pexrav component added here is unabsorbed and not subjected to the same absorption as the primary power-law. To treat this correctly, the pexrav spectrum would need to be added to the intrinsic emission of the Monte-Carlo models that calculate the effect of Compton scattering within the torus model, which is not easily done. The use of the torus model here to determine the opening angle of the torus is fairly novel, as is the use of X-ray torus models to understand high-energy emission in heavily obscured AGN. Therefore, in Section sec_models we compared the spectral fit parameters determined from the classical pexrav model and the more recent mytorus model when fitted to simulated spectra from the torus model for a range of \ and . Here, we compare the fits to real \ data with the torus model presented here to those made with pexrav and mytorus from previous published works. We list the different \ and values obtained from each in Table table_speccomp . In our initial analysis, we found that pexrav systematically underestimates with respect to both torus and mytorus , most likely because it cannot reproduce the full strength of the Compton hump produced by a torus geometry. This is also found in our comparison of fits to real data, as fits with pexrav produce a lower than both the torus or mytorus model for NGC 424, NGC 1068, NGC 1320 and IC 2560. As for fits with mytorus with respect to the torus model, the measurements are consistent with each other within the uncertainties, with the exceptions of NGC 1068 and NGC 4945. For NGC 1068, use a large, combined set of X-ray spectral data from different observatories with far more detailed spectral modelling than is done here, including modelling of the host spectrum. Futhermore, the mytorus fits are done in decoupled mode. For NGC 4945, the torus model measures 0.2 lower than mytorus . However, the measurement of using mytorus in was done on a time-resolved basis with mytorus in decoupled mode, where the parameters of the reflection components are not fixed to those of the transmitted component. However, we do measure a lower \ for NGC 4945 than the mytorus model, which may be the reason for the discrepancy. Furthermore, a fit with the sphere model produces a better fit to the data and a value consistent with mytorus (1.78 ^ +0.13 _ 0.06 ). For Mrk 34, the difference in the measured values between the two models is 0.5 , with mytorus producing the steeper slope. The torus model, however, measures >10^ 25 \ in Mrk 34, whereas mytorus is limited to <10^ 25 , and, indeed the upper limit is reached in the fit. The inability of mytorus to measure columns greater than this is the likely cause of disagreement in Mrk 34, as also noted by . The two measured values are nevertheless consistent within the large uncertainties in .

sources.summary: The paper studies Mrk 34 as a disturbed type II quasar host with an extended outflow, finding young stellar populations near the blue outflow edge and preventive feedback elsewhere.

quotes.quote: Previous studies of Mrk 34 demonstrate that it is a Compton-thick gandhi14 quasar at a redshift z = 0.0501 with an [ OIII ] luminosity of L _ [ OIII ] = 10^ 8.85 L_ reyes08 . The quasar is hosted by an Sa-type spiral galaxy which also has a weak bar structure nair10 , position angle = 30 , inclination i = 41 , and an ellipticity of e = 1-b/a = 0.25 fischer18 . Deep imaging studies have also shown that Mrk 34 is morphologically disturbed zhao19,pierce21 , demonstrating recent merger activity.

MRK 34 presents an interesting laboratory in which to test the relationship between AGN driven outflows and star formation. It is a system that hosts a large-scale outflow that is in the plane of the disc, producing the maximum coupling potential between the outflow and ISM, whilst at the same time, appearing to be undergoing suppressed star-formation in the nuclear region. Is this because the outflow is actively suppressing the star formation? Figure contours once again shows the proportion of YSP flux in the normalising bin, with the left panel overlayed with contours of V02 (blue) and V98 (red), whilst the right panel is overlayed with the W80 contours (velocities given in caption). Comparing the kinematics of the outflowing gas with the distribution of the YSP, a relative enhancement is clearly coincident at the edge of the V02 contours, suggesting that a region of enhanced star-formation is coincident with the outer edge of the outflow.

sources.summary: The paper resolves hard X-ray emission in Mrk 34, identifying high-velocity Fe K features within 200 pc that may connect ultrafast nuclear outflows to larger-scale AGN feedback.

quotes.quote: We present Chandra ACIS imaging spectroscopy of the nucleus of the Seyfert 2 Galaxy Mrk 34. We identify spatially and spectrally resolved features in the band that includes Fe K , Fe 25 and Fe 26 . These features indicate high-velocity ( 15,000 km s ^ 1 line-of-sight) material spanning 0 5 , within 200 pc of the nucleus. This outflow could have deprojected velocities 12-28 greater than the [ O 3 ] emitting outflows, and could potentially dominate the kinetic power in the outflow. This emission may point to the origins of the optical and X-ray winds observed at larger radii, and could indicate a link between ultra-fast outflows and AGN feedback on kpc scales.

Mrk 34 is highly obscured (log [ n_ H / cm ^ 2 ] 24.3 ; Gandhi14,Zhao20 ), and is therefore another excellent candidate for similar spatial mapping of extended hard emission. In this paper, we examine spatially resolved emission in Chandra observations of Mrk 34 in the 5.5-7.0 keV band. We find evidence for high-velocity iron outflows which may provide a link between ultrafast outflows seen in absorption and kpc-scale winds seen in emission.

sources.summary: The paper maps Mrk 34 as a distant Seyfert with FIR and maser activity, aligned optical and radio emission, cone-like excitation, strong Seyfert diagnostics, and S-shaped gas velocities.

quotes.quote: Mrk 34 is known to be a luminous FIR source with a powerful water maser HenkelMrk34 . Optical line emission was found to be spatially correlated with radio continuum emission, PA 150 165 Nagar99Orient,Falcke98,Ulve84V . Mrk 34 is the most distant object in our OASIS sample and in this case our FOV corresponds to the central 5 kpc. Because of the high redshift of the galaxy, the [ S ii ] doublet is located outside the observed spectral range, and we are therefore unable to infer all of the spectral-diagnostic ratios, and the electron density. The observed emission in all of the observed lines has a similar morphology, extended along the direction of PA 140 150 (Fig. Mrk34maps ).

The spatially resolved diagnostic diagrams indicate that the emission corresponds to a strong Seyfert regime. The map of the mean LOS velocities in H (Fig. Mrk34vel ) provides an example of twisted S-shape isocontours.

sources.summary: The paper models Mrk 34 as a Seyfert 2 with an S-shaped NLR, attributing the morphology to bicone-disk intersection and possible warped-disk geometry.

quotes.quote: Mrk 34 This is a Seyfert 2 galaxy with a backwards S-shape NLR, as shown in Figure mrk34 , similar to Mrk 3 Cre10b . The modeled kinematics provide a good fit to the data, matching well to all four components save for a few high-velocity points. Employing our geometric model, we see that the S-shaped formation is likely an intersection between a gas spiral in the host disk and the ionization bicone as the projected area of the host disk enclosed by the outer opening angle matches well with emission seen in the [ O III ] imaging. Similar to Mrk 573 Fis10 , we suggest rotation does not play a role in the kinematics we observe as the low velocity components would correspond to a disk rotating clockwise while the host disk spiral arms are winding up in the counterclockwise direction. Kon06 identified water maser emission in Mrk 34 due to an edge-on accretion disk.

Thus, because our model gives the inclination of the bicone axis to be 25 ^ out of the plane of the sky, it is possible that Mrk 34 experiences the same warped disk scenario as Circinus. Alternatively, if a torus is collimating the ionizing radiation, it is tilted 25 ^ with respect to the maser disk.

sources.summary: The paper models Markarian 34 as the nearest Compton-thick QSO2, finding complex nuclear rotation and outflow, ionized gas extending to 2 kpc, and energetic NLR feedback.

quotes.quote: We present spatially resolved mass outflow rate measurements ( M_ out ) for the narrow line region of Markarian 34, the nearest Compton-thick type 2 quasar (QSO2). Spectra obtained with the Hubble Space Telescope and at Apache Point Observatory reveal complex kinematics, with distinct signatures of outflow and rotation within 2 kpc of the nucleus. Using multi-component photoionization models, we find that the outflow contains a total ionized gas mass of M 1.6 10^6 M_ . Combining this with the kinematics yields a peak outflow rate of M_ out 2.0 0.4 M_ yr ^ 1 at a distance of 470 pc from the nucleus, with a spatially integrated kinetic energy of E 1.4 10^ 55 erg.

We selected the type 2 quasar (QSO2) Markarian 34 (Mrk 34, SDSS J103408.58+600152.1, MCG+10-15-104) to extend our sample to higher redshifts, bolometric luminosities, and outflow radii, complementing our studies in and . The central AGN resides in an Sa-type galaxy with spiral arms and a weak bar structure nair2010 that are visible in Figure structure . Using our observations, we derive ( 3.3) a new heliocentric redshift of z = 0.05080 , a recessional velocity of 14,843 km s ^ 1 , a Hubble distance of 209 Mpc, and a spatial scale of 1014 pc arcsec ^ 1 , assuming H_0 = 71 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper presents Mrk 34 as a distant Seyfert 2 FIR source with extended radio emission and one of the most luminous H2O megamasers then detected.

quotes.quote: Mrk 34 (IRAS 10309+6017), another luminous ( 10 ^ 11 L _ ) FIR source, is a distant Seyfert 2 galaxy ( z =0.0505, D 200 Mpc; Falcke et al. 1998). The optical galaxy is characterized as having an inclination angle of 57 \ in Whittle (1992), though a second generation Digital Sky Survey (DSS) image shows the galaxy to be compact, with poorly defined outer isophotes (Nagar Wilson 1999). The radio emission has an extended structure ( 2 5; Ulvestad Wilson 1984), and strong evidence for an interaction between the radio jet and NLR clouds has been found by Falcke et al. (1998).

Mrk 34 is one of the most distant and most luminous H _2 O megamasers ever detected. The maser shows two or three distinct spectral features (Figs. mrk34a and mrk34b ). One is centered at a velocity of 14840 km s ^ 1 , another at 15770 km s ^ 1 , and a third one is tentatively seen at 14665 km s ^ 1 . Peak flux densities are up to 10 mJy and total isotropic luminosities are 1000 L _ .

sources.summary: Mrk 34 is described as a biconical, Z-shaped QSO2 whose [O III] kinematics include high-velocity outflows, line splitting, and a 1.50 kpc outflow radius.

quotes.quote: Mrk 34 The [ O III ] morphology in Mrk 34 is a clear, biconical structure, with a 'Z' shape likely due to the illumination of spiral arms in the host disk, similar to previously observed targets like Mrk 3 Cre10b and Mrk 573 Fis10,Fis17 . [ O III ] kinematics across the three separate slit positions show high-velocity ( 1000 km s ^ 1 ) outflows existing inside projected distances of 500 pc. Extended kinematics for the rest of the system appear to be largely rotational, however large deviations from rotation and emission-line splitting are present on either end of the nucleus out to 1.5 kpc.

Here, we observe line splitting with both lines deviating from the rotation curve in opposite directions, to an approximate maximum difference of 500 km s ^ 1 . Regions that display line splitting are observed as the outer arcs being illuminated in the NLR, suggesting that these kinematics are due to material in the spiral arms being driven or ablated in directions perpendicular to the radial outflows. We define R _ out to encompass the entirety of the observed kinematics with multiple components for a radial distance of 1.50 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 34 has the sample’s largest outflow energetics, but would need about ten times more [O III] emitting gas for efficient feedback.

quotes.quote: For Mrk 34, which has the largest kinetic luminosity of our sample, in order to produce efficient feedback there would have to be 10 times more [ O III ] emitting gas than we detected at its position of maximum kinetic luminosity.

Specifically for Mrk 34, Ga2014 were able to determine the X-ray luminosity, L_ (2-10keV) = 9( 3) 10^ 43 erg. s^ 1 , which makes it possible to calculate Q for this target. They did not detect any significant variability between their NuSTAR and XMM-Newton observations. Therefore, we do not consider the possible variability of the X-ray source.

We choose Mrk 34 since, among the targets in our sample, it is the target that shows the highest outflow rate and most extended outflows. We find that, in order to reach the lower limit of E / L_ bol = 0.5 , Mrk 34 would have to possess an outflow rate of 20 M yr^ 1 , in contrast to our measured value of 1.97^ +5.9 _ 1.5 M yr^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper models Mrk 34 as a nearby QSO2 whose extended outflows may require X-ray winds to disturb and entrain [O III] gas.

quotes.quote: We use Mrk 477 and Mrk 34 in order to test our models against recent HST STIS observations of [ O III ] emission-line kinematics, since these AGNs possess more energetic outflows than found in Seyfert galaxies. We find that the outflows within 500 pc are consistent with radiative acceleration of dusty gas, however the outflows in Mrk 34 are significantly more extended and may not be directly accelerated by radiation. We characterise the properties of X-ray winds found from the expansion of [ O III ] emitting gas close to the black hole. We show that such winds possess the kinetic energy density to disturb [ O III ] gas at 1.8 kpc, and have sufficient energy to entrain the [ O III ] clouds at 1.2 kpc. Assuming that the X-ray wind possesses the same radial mass distribution as the [ O III ] gas, we find that the peak kinetic luminosity for this wind is 2 of Mrk 34's bolometric luminosity, which is in the 0.5 5 range required by some models for efficient feedback.
In the case of Mrk 34 the two components correspond to a pseudo-bulge and a disk.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Mrk34 as a database-classified galaxy, a known Compton-thick AGN in the literature, and one of two known Compton-thick AGN recovered outside the Swift BAT sample.

quotes.quote: Among these 341 sources only 3 (IRAS06190+1040, IRAS21144+5430, Mrk34) are classified as a galaxy in the searched databases. Mrk34 is found to be a already known CT AGN in the literature Gandhi2014 .
As a result of this criteria we find two known CT AGN (NGC4418 and Mrk34) that are not included in Swift BAT sample.

gmw_00006724.name: FIRST J120041.4+314745
gmw_00006724.overview: FIRST J120041.4+314745 is an active galaxy featuring a highly extended [O III] emission region [arXiv:1802.06184, arXiv:2010.08050] and high bolometric luminosity [arXiv:2010.08050]. The system exhibits high-velocity inner gas outflows [arXiv:1802.06184] and appears to be interacting with a smaller satellite galaxy to its southwest [arXiv:1802.06184].

sources.summary: FIRST J120041 has the sample's largest projected [O III] extent, high-velocity inner emission, disturbed gas beyond the slit, and evidence of interaction with a satellite galaxy.

quotes.quote: FIRST J120041 FIRST J120041 has the largest projected [ O III ] morphology extent in our sample, with an R _ max of 6 kpc, which is on the same scale as the extent of the observable host galaxy in SDSS imaging. [ O III ] velocities are measured on much smaller scales, extending to distances between 0.8 - 1.5 kpc from the nucleus, which can be mapped to the two bright lobes on either side of the nucleus along the STIS slit. Multi-component and high-velocity emission lines are observed in the inner 0.5'', for an R _ out of 1 kpc, with high FWHM, single-component emission lines detected to the furthest extent of our measurements (R _ dist = 1.12 kpc). Outflows and disturbed kinematics may reach to further distances outside the area sampled by the STIS slit, as the bright knots where we observe the high-velocity gas extend to a radius of 0.76 '' , or 1.55 kpc. This target appears to be part of an ongoing or recent interaction with a smaller, satellite galaxy to the southwest, as filamentary structure can be observed linking the two systems.

sources.summary: The paper describes FIRST J120041.4+314745 as having very extended [O III] emission, high bolometric luminosity and radiation pressure, but modest outflow energetics.

quotes.quote: In regard to the morphology of the sample, F2018 found that in some of the targets the [ O III ] region is very extended, such as in FIRST J120041.4+314745, which has a maximum radial extent in its [ O III ] image, R_ max , of 5.92 kpc.

The radiation pressure force from the bolometric luminosity, L_ bol , ranges from 5.4^ +16.2 _ 4.0 10^ 34 dyne, for SDSS J115245.66+101623.8, to 1.9^ +5.7 _ 1.4 10^ 35 dyne, for FIRST J120041.4+314745. The peak momentum flow rate extends from 7.5^ +22.5 _ 5.6 10^ 30 dyne, for SDSS J115245.66+101623.8, to 2.8^ +8.4 _ 2.1 10^ 34 dyne, for Mrk 34.

gmw_00006725.name: 2MASX J13003807+5454367
gmw_00006725.overview: The active galaxy 2MASX J13003807+5454367 is classified as a type 2 quasar [arXiv:2010.08050] that exhibits extended [O III] emission arcs [arXiv:1802.06184, arXiv:2010.08050]. These arcs and a linear nuclear feature likely represent spiral arms illuminated by the central engine [arXiv:1802.06184], which drives a compact 160-parsec outflow [arXiv:1802.06184, arXiv:2010.08050].

sources.summary: 2MASX J130038 has extended [O III] arcs and a linear nuclear feature interpreted as illuminated spiral arms, mostly undisturbed rotation, and a compact 160 pc outflow.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J130038 The [ O III ] structure of this target is extended, with two arcs of bright emission and a linear feature near the nucleus, suggesting that the observed morphology is due to spiral arms being illuminated by the central engine. The kinematics sampled by our STIS observations agree with this interpretation, as they appear to be largely in kinematically un-disturbed rotation. Velocities are blueshifted to the southeast and redshifted to the northwest, with FWHMs ranging between 100 - 200 km s ^ 1 . A compact, broad FWHM component is observed immediately northwest of the nucleus, with R _ out 160 pc, suggesting outflows are being driven off the linear filament near the nucleus observed in imaging, similar to Mrk 573. As single-component emission lines at greater radii possess FWHMs < 250 km s ^ 1 , R _ out and R _ dist are the same distance.

sources.summary: The paper groups 2MASX J13003807+5454367 with QSO2s showing extended [O III] emission but compact, weak outflow regions and possible recent high-state activity.

quotes.quote: When comparing the two plots, we find three AGNs, SDSS J115245.66+101623.8, 2MASX J13003807+5454367 and B2 1435+30, are separate from the remainder of the QSO2 sample. Their shift in positions from the left panel to the right panel on Figure fig:Fig_11 is due to their very extended [ O III ] emission, based on the ACS images, despite having very weak outflow rates.
Our calculations show that for SDSS J115245.66+101623.8, 2MASX J13003807+5454367 and B2 1435+30 these values are 230, 270 and 310 years ago, respectively.

gmw_00006726.name: B2 1435+30
gmw_00006726.overview: B2 1435+30 is an active galaxy that appears to be part of a recent galaxy interaction with an eastern satellite [arXiv:1802.06184]. It features very extended [O III] emission [arXiv:2010.08050] with a biconical morphology [arXiv:1802.06184] and hosts a compact, weak nuclear gas outflow [arXiv:1802.06184, arXiv:2010.08050].

sources.summary: B2 1435+30 has biconical [O III] emission, nuclear high-FWHM outflowing gas, disturbed rotation through the southwest cone, and evidence of a recent interaction.

quotes.quote: B2 1435+30 The [ O III ] morphology of B2 1435+30 is biconical, with a compact arc to the east, and a bright, extended cone to the west, which are both traced kinematically by our STIS observations. This target does not possess significantly high velocities or multiple components in its kinematics, however, we find that the redshifted, high FWHM ( 700 km s ^ 1 ) gas kinematics over the nucleus do not follow the overall rotation pattern of the surrounding gas at greater radii and deem these kinematics to be in outflow, with R _ out = 170 pc. Kinematics across the features outside the nucleus resemble kinematically disturbed rotation, observed as 400 km s ^ 1 FWHM gas, extending throughout the southwest cone, with R _ dist > 1.5 kpc. This target appears to be part of a recent galaxy interaction, with extended structure in SDSS imaging linking it to an eastern satellite galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper finds B2 1435+30 has very extended [O III] emission but among the weakest outflow rates, making it one of three compact-outflow exceptions.

quotes.quote: When comparing the two plots, we find three AGNs, SDSS J115245.66+101623.8, 2MASX J13003807+5454367 and B2 1435+30, are separate from the remainder of the QSO2 sample. Their shift in positions from the left panel to the right panel on Figure fig:Fig_11 is due to their very extended [ O III ] emission, based on the ACS images, despite having very weak outflow rates.
Our calculations show that for SDSS J115245.66+101623.8, 2MASX J13003807+5454367 and B2 1435+30 these values are 230, 270 and 310 years ago, respectively.

The peak in the mass outflow rate peaks from 9.3^ +27.9 _ 7.0 10^ 3 M yr^ 1 , for B2 1435+30, to 10.3^ +30.9 _ 7.7 M yr^ 1 , for Mrk 34 (Figure fig:Fig_5 ), at a distance varying from 100 pc to 1,260 pc from the nucleus (see Table tab:Table_4 ) and then decrease at larger distances.

gmw_00006727.name: SDSS J120601.69+514227.8
gmw_00006727.overview: SDSS J120601.69+514227.8 is a strongly lensed star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z=2 [arXiv:1010.1538]. It exhibits an integrated star formation rate of 50 solar masses per year and displays a strong radial metallicity gradient [arXiv:1010.1538].

sources.summary: The paper maps the lensed z=2 Clone arc, measuring its velocity field, star formation, dynamical mass, and steep metallicity gradient as evidence for inside-out growth.

quotes.quote: We present near-infrared imaging spectroscopy of the strongly-lensed z=2.00 galaxy SDSS J120601.69+514227.8 (`the Clone arc'). Using OSIRIS on the Keck 2 telescope with laser guide star adaptive optics, we achieve resolved spectroscopy with 0.20 arcsecond FWHM resolution in the diagnostic emission lines [ O iii ] , H , and [ N ii ] . The lensing magnification allows us to map the velocity and star formation from H emission at a physical resolution of 300 pc in the galaxy source plane. With an integrated star formation rate of 50 M_ yr ^ 1 , the galaxy is typical of sources similarly studied at this epoch.

The Clone arc is evidently representative of UV-bright star-forming galaxies at z 2 both in its integrated star formation rate of 50 M_ yr ^ 1 and its resolved kinematics. Via the detailed study of 3 emission lines, we have found a strong radial gradient in both the [ N ii ] /H and [ O iii ] /H flux ratios, consistent with a metallicity gradient which we measure to be d O/H dR = -0.27 0.05 dex/kpc.

gmw_00006728.name: GOODS J033238.76-275121.6
gmw_00006728.overview: GOODS J033238.76-275121.6 is an obscured, borderline quasar at a redshift of approximately 3.045 [arXiv:1302.2623]. The galaxy features a spatially asymmetric Lyman-alpha emission line halo [arXiv:1302.2623].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GOODS J033238.76-275121.6 as an obscured, borderline QSO at z about 3.045 with an asymmetric Ly-alpha halo spatially aligned with a nearby double-lobed galaxy.

quotes.quote: The slit happened to intersect at least partly the object known as GOODS J033238.76-275121.6 (Giavalisco et al 2004) or Combo-17 26280 (Wolf et al 2001), which has an absorption-corrected, rest-frame X-ray (0.5-8 keV) luminosity of 9.22 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 (Xue et al 2011). Taken together with its hardness ratio and red optical colors this suggests classification as a type 2 AGN or obscured, borderline QSO (e.g., Szokoly et al 2004; Padovani et al 2004). A range of redshift estimates exist in the literature (see table 1), including a spectroscopic redshift of z=3.951 (Vanzella et 2010).

In our spectrum the QSO has a spatially asymmetric Ly emission line peaking near 4920 , or at a redshift of 3.049. The presence and wavelength position of faint CIV emission suggests a redshift 3.045, which is lower by about 300 kms ^ 1 .

gmw_00006729.overview: 03521 + 0028 is a close-pair galaxy system [arXiv:astro-ph/0508557] classified as both a starburst and a LINER [arXiv:astro-ph/0508557]. The system is an OH megamaser [arXiv:astro-ph/0508557] that lacks a visible CO turnover, leaving its molecular gas properties unconstrained [arXiv:2402.17851].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 03521 + 0028 as a close-pair OH megamaser with starburst and LINER classifications, uniquely detected in Chandra X-rays with a very low spurious-detection probability.

quotes.quote: 03521 + 0028 : this source is comprised of a close pair; both components, separated by 1.3 \ (corresponding to 3.4 kpc), are visible in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) but not in the DPOSS2. This object has been classified as a starburst according to the ISO spectrum (Lutz, Veilleux Genzel 1999) and a low-ionisation nuclear emission-line region galaxy (LINER) according to the optical spectrum (Nagar et al. 2003).

IRAS FSC 03521 + 0028 is the only detected source (filled triangle in Fig. 1), with 5 counts in the observed 0.5--8 keV band; we note that this is also the source with the highest exposure by \ (see Table 1). Given the adopted threshold and the small number of pixels being searched due to the known source positions and the sub-arcsec on-axis angular resolution of , the probability that this detection is spurious is extremely low ( 8 10^ 6 ).

sources.summary: The paper says IRAS 03521+0028 lacks a visible CO turnover, preventing H2 gas-property constraints and leaving its CO ladder shape highly uncertain.

quotes.quote: Already from a visual inspection, we can observe sources with SLEDs peaking at J_ up 3,4 (e.g., IRAS F05189-2524, IRAS 09022-3615, IRAS F12112+0305, IRAS F13451+1232, IRAS 17208-0014, IRAS 19254-7245, IRAS F20551-4250 and IRAS F23128-5919), others showing a plateau-like feature from J_ up 3 (IRAS F01572+0009 and IRAS F23060+0505), and lastly, sources for which no plateau nor turn-over point is observed up to the highest detected CO transition (IRAS 03521+0028, IRAS 06035-7102, IRAS 06206-6315, IRAS 08311-2459, IRAS 10378+1109, IRAS 16090-0139 and IRAS F22491-1808).
For source IRAS 03521+0028, with only three detected CO ( J_ up 4 ) lines and no visible turn-over, we cannot constrain the H _2 gas properties.

There is only one source for which such classification is not possible, i.e., IRAS 03521+0028, for which we only have data up to CO(4--3) and no detection of a clear peak, leaving the shape of the CO ladder highly uncertain.

gmw_00006730.name: NGC 4710
gmw_00006730.overview: NGC 4710 is an edge-on lenticular galaxy in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:1611.09547, arXiv:1604.07624] that features a boxy-peanut or X-shaped bulge [arXiv:1611.09547, arXiv:1603.02546] and a central asymmetric dust lane [arXiv:2210.12255]. The galaxy contains molecular gas [arXiv:1709.09300], exhibits ongoing star formation [arXiv:1709.09300], and hosts centrally peaked methanol emission [arXiv:1305.2328].

sources.summary: The paper's decomposition of NGC 4710 finds an extremely tiny exponential bulge, a bar with X-shaped residuals, possible ring or ansae structures, and concludes bulge rotation cannot be measured cleanly.

quotes.quote: Figure 2 shows the results from the decomposition of NGC 4710. The best model includes an exponential bulge with bulge-to-total ratio B/T=0.001 , which suggests this can in fact be a bulgeless galaxy. The structure coming out of the disk plane is a bar, which is included in the model. The X-shape in the residual image strongly corroborates the presence of a bar. Also seen in the residual image are two thin structures at each side of the galaxy center, which are likely to be a ring (pseudo? broken?), or ansae at the bar ends.

Interestingly, NGC 4710 is classified in NED as a possibly ringed, unbarred S0 galaxy. Thus, in the case of NGC 4710, the structural analysis uncover that we cannot measure the rotation of the tiny exponential bulge (if it indeed exists), but only of the disk and the bar. In most other cases, we estimated the bulge rotation velocity from measurements within one bulge effective radius, where the bulge light dominates over other present components. For a few galaxies, as in the case of NGC 4710, we did not make such estimation, as we deemed that either the bulge was too small to properly measure its rotation with the current data, or the spectra are too much contaminated by light from a bar and/or a thick disk.

sources.summary: The paper uses MUSE observations of NGC 4710 to show its old boxy/peanut bulge has a peanut-shaped metallicity map, supporting kinematic fractionation by a bar without a classical bulge.

quotes.quote: In this context, NGC 4710 is one of the best laboratories to test this prediction. NGC 4710, located at 16.9 Mpc , has a stellar mass of M_ 7 10^ 10 M_ Cappellari+13 , is classified as an exactly edge-on barred galaxy with a B/PS bulge buta+15 , and has no co-spatial large-scale classical bulge gadotti+12, gonzalez+16 . Furthermore, the arms of its X-shaped bulge structure are seen very prominently, as a consequence of a nearly side-on orientation of the bar buta+15 . In this Letter we investigate the stellar population properties of the B/PS bulge of NGC 4710, directly testing the prediction of kinematic fractionation.

The most striking feature of the mass-weighted metallicity map of NGC 4710 is its peanut shape, that is, the vertical metallicity gradient has a different pattern along the minor axis than at the sides of the B/PS bulge. The metal-rich population extends to larger heights away from the plane at intermediate distances ( 1.5 kpc) from the center than it does along the minor axis. We refer to this gradient pattern as the peanut-shaped metallicity map of NGC 4710.
This shows a remarkable qualitative similarity to the metallicity map of NGC 4710, both in terms of shape and of vertical metallicity gradient.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 4710's mid-plane map is compromised by quadrupolar and dipolar signals, a central double peak linked to asymmetric dust lanes, and damaged image columns.

quotes.quote: For NGC 3628, NGC 4710, NGC 5073, and NGC 5529 the mid-plane asymmetry maps are strongly affected by a quadrupolar signal (which affects at least half the extent of the galaxy itself). This signal implies that we were unable to correctly identify the disc PA, despite our careful analysis. Visual inspection of the S ^4 G images of these galaxies and corresponding unsharp masked images (Fig.  fig:sample , middle panels) identifies strong and intrinsically asymmetric features in the discs (such as warps and/or lopsided discs) which may have affected the measurement of the disc PA.

NGC 4710 hosts a double peak near the centre, which may produce the small dipolar signal in the central region of the mid-plane asymmetry map. This feature is associated with an asymmetric dust lane, on each side of the centre, as suggested by . The outer regions of the disc appear more regular, despite a weak quadrupolar signal visible at |x| 75 arcsec. Moreover, the image of NGC 4710 suffers from columns of pixels with damaged/missing signal, affecting its entire left side. Masking this region results in not being able to build effective mid-plane asymmetry diagnostics.

sources.summary: The paper uses MUSE to map NGC 4710, finding Milky-Way-like bulge kinematics, a pronounced boxy-peanut/X-shaped bulge, and a vertical metallicity gradient.

quotes.quote: We used the new ESO VLT instrument MUSE to obtain spectral and imaging coverage of NGC 4710. The wide area and excellent sampling of the MUSE integral field spectrograph allows us to investigate the dynamical properties of the X-shaped bulge of NGC 4710 and compare it with the properties of the Milky Way's own X-shaped bulge. We measured the radial velocities, velocity dispersion, and stellar populations using a penalized pixel full spectral fitting technique adopting simple stellar populations models, on a 1' 1' area centred on the bulge of NGC 4710. We have constructed the velocity maps of the bulge of NGC 4710 and we investigated the presence of vertical metallicity gradients.

We find the line-of-sight velocity maps and 1D rotation curves of the bulge of NGC 4710 to be remarkably similar to those of the Milky Way bulge. Some specific differences that were identified are in good agreement with the expectations from variations in the bar orientation angle. The bulge of NGC 4710 has a boxy-peanut morphology with a pronounced X-shape, showing no indication of any additional spheroidally distributed bulge population, in which we measure a vertical metallicity gradient of 0.35 dex/kpc.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4710 as a Virgo-cluster S0-a with distinct radial regions, a thick disc lacking strong age or metallicity gradients, and a complex evolutionary history.

quotes.quote: 4710 Our data extends to 85 arcsec (6.8 kpc) and we can identify at least three distinct regions in 4710 by radius: (i) an inner dusty region within 30 arcsec that corresponds to the outer edge of a clearly seen X-shaped structure, (ii) a bright thin blue disc extending to 60 arcsec, and (iii) weaker outer regions.

At the same time, the thick disc of 4710 (at 560 pc above the mid-plane) possesses significant gradients neither in age nor in metallicity, which coincide with the values in both outer ( >60 arcsec) and inner ( <30 arcsec) regions in the mid-plane, t 4 5 Gyr, [ Fe/H ] \ 0.15 dex.

The apparent multi-component structure in the mid-plane of 4710 suggests a complex evolution history. We have to keep in mind that this galaxy belongs to the Virgo cluster. Despite of the fact that it is nowadays located at a significant distance from 87 ( 1.6 Mpc), we have a reason to believe that 4710 already passed the cluster center in the past. According to Koopmann2001 , there is an abrupt truncation of GALEX UV and H _ fluxes, tracers of recent and ongoing star formation, beyond =60 arcsec.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC4710 for rotation diagrams, finding centrally peaked methanol emission, a smaller methanol line width, and a higher methanol rotation temperature than CO/CS.

quotes.quote: Figure NGC4710rotdiag shows an example (for the source where we detect the most transitions) of the rotation diagrams derived for each molecule where we detect at least two lines of that species.

NGC4710 also has centrally peaked methanol emission, in this case with a smaller line width than the other transitions. This could indicate that some additional mechanism (e.g. shocks) is liberating additional methanol in the central parts of these objects (which both have bars).
In NGC4710 the methanol transitions suggest a rotation temperature of 8.5 K, larger than the CO/CS rotation temperatures.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4710 as an early-type galaxy with molecular gas, ongoing star formation, young mean stellar age indicators, and substantial young-star or extended star-formation-history components in model fits.

quotes.quote: IC 0719, NGC 3032, NGC 3156, NGC 3182, NGC 3489 and NGC 4710 were shown to contain molecular gas combes_molecular_2007, young_atlas3d_2014 , corresponding to regions of ongoing star formation davis_atlas3d_2014 , and have young mean stellar ages.

Reddening case Regardless of wavelength range, BC03 identifies post-starbursts NGC 3032 and NGC 3156 as young, and NGC 4710 as containing a substantial fraction of young stars. Both wavelengths also require smaller fractions of young stars in NGC 3098, NGC 3301, NGC 3489 and NGC 5475.

Use of the M11 models in the near-infrared also leads to young ages for NGC 3032 and NGC 4710, in agreement with the optical, however the post-starburst galaxy NGC 3156 appears old in this case. As with BC03, M11 models require small fractions of young stars in NGC 3098, NGC 3301, NGC 3489, NGC 4608 and NGC 5475. The remainder of the galaxies appear old at both wavelengths. The V16 near-infrared fits imply a small burst in NGC 3032, atop a pre-existing old population, which is the most physically likely scenario. NGC 3156 and NGC 4710 also have extended SFHs, however the mass-weighted mean ages derived using V16 are all old ( t  8yr), contrary to the optical SFHs, which span a large range of mean ages.

gmw_00006731.name: 4C29.41
gmw_00006731.overview: The radio galaxy 4C29.41 is the brightest cluster galaxy of Abell 1213 [arXiv:0908.4391, arXiv:0909.0911], where it forms part of a central double galaxy system [arXiv:0908.4391, arXiv:0909.0911]. It exhibits a small Fanaroff-Riley II double radio structure [arXiv:2303.02528, arXiv:0909.0911] with a one-sided parsec-scale jet [arXiv:0908.4391].

sources.summary: The paper associates 1113+29 with the BCG of Abell 1213, describes its double radio structure and cluster X-ray emission, and detects a one-sided parsec jet.

quotes.quote: 1113+29 (4C 29.41) -- This radio source is associated with the BCG of Abell 1213, which is part of a double system of galaxies close to the center of the cluster ( ). At radio frequencies, it shows a double structure with linear size 61 kpc and a total radio power at 1.4 GHz Log P_ 1.4 =24.7 ( ). An extended weak X-ray emission has been found to be associated with the cluster ( ). In our VLBA image, the source shows an extended one-sided jet (Fig. f1113.1 ), resolved in the high resolution image and suggestive of a limb-brightened structure (Fig. f1113.2 ). The parsec-scale jet is on the same side as the main kiloparsec scale jet. The total correlated flux in the VLBA image is 40 mJy, and therefore no arcsecond core flux is missing on the parsec scale.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C29.41 as a central FR II radio galaxy in A1213, near diffuse emission whose spectral index favors relic reacceleration of fossil electrons.

quotes.quote: This is a poor (Abell richness class=1; Abell et al. ) cluster at z 0.047 dominated in its central region by the FR II radio galaxy 4C29.41 and two more cluster members identified as radio galaxies. Very notably, NE of 4C29.41, data of the NRAO VLA Sky Survey (NVSS; Condon et al. ) and observations with the Very Large Array (VLA) at 1.4 GHz by Giovannini et al. ( ; hereafter G09) revealed the presence of a diffuse extended emission that does not seem due to the discrete radio sources in the cluster central region.
The archival observation was pointed on the radio galaxy 4C29.41, whose optical counterpart is ID 467=BG1 (obs. ID 0550270101).

Our VLA 1.4 GHz/LOFAR 144 MHz spectral index map rejects the hypothesis of the radio galaxy tail and supports a different explanation. In fact, the spectral index distribution (Sect. lofar and Fig. figrsi ) is compatible with a radio relic interpretation, where ``fossil'' electrons of the radio galaxy 4C29.41 (but also of the head-tail galaxy ID 442) are reaccelerated by shock(s) due to a merger.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C29.41 as the dumbbell radio-galaxy BCG in Abell 1213, with a small FR II-like double morphology distinct from the cluster's diffuse radio halo.

quotes.quote: Abell 1213 is a poor nearby Abell cluster at z = 0.0469, characterized by the presence of a dumbell radio galaxy (the BCG) identified with a double radio source (4C29.41, B2 1113+29, ).
The radio morphology of the BCG is peculiar being a small-size bright double similar to FR II radio galaxies.
The radio structure of 4C29.41 and the size of the diffuse component excludes a possible connection between the BCG and the diffuse structure due to a possible proper motion of the BCG.

gmw_00006732.name: SDSS J163631.47+475131.8
gmw_00006732.overview: SDSS J163631.47+475131.8 is a galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.36 [arXiv:2310.14310]. It is identified as the host galaxy of the gamma-ray burst GRB 230812B and has a low star formation rate [arXiv:2310.14310].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J163631.47+475131.8 as the GRB 230812B host, derives z=0.36, estimates host fluxes, and models its mass, SFR, and attenuation.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of GRB 230812B is SDSS J163631.47+475131.8, with measurements available in SDSS DR16 sdssdr16 , but its photometry there is marked as unreliable. The host galaxy's redshift z=0.36 was determined through GTC spectroscopic observations of emission lines 2023GCN.34409....1D . We studied its brightness, both for host flux subtraction and spectral analysis.

Using these host flux contributions as approximations for the observed magnitude of the galaxy as a whole, we apply the CIGALE https://cigale.lam.fr/ code cigale to study the spectral energy distribution (SED) of the galaxy. This analysis constrains the model parameter space to a mass M = (1.99 0.54) 10^9 M_ , a star formation rate (on the last 10 Myr) SFR = 0.17 0.07 M_ yr ^ 1 , and an attenuation A_V = 0.09 0.06 mag.

gmw_00006733.name: Haro 11
gmw_00006733.overview: Haro 11 is a starburst luminous blue compact galaxy and a merger system with kinematics resembling the Antennae galaxy [arXiv:1903.11662]. Its ionized gas is arranged in structures like superbubbles, filaments, and arcs powered by central star-forming knots and a dusty arm [arXiv:1903.11662].

sources.summary: The paper studies Haro 11 as a starburst blue compact merger with LyC leakage, young massive clusters, complex ionized gas, feedback-driven structures, and possible escaping ionized gas.

quotes.quote: The galaxy studied in this paper is Haro 11, a well-known starburst luminous blue compact galaxy. Haro 11 is a \ emitter Hayes2007 and one of few galaxies where Lyman continuum has been detected Bergvall2006,Leitet2011 . In contrast to most BCGs, its ionized gas mass is larger than its neutral gas content MacHattie2014,pardy2016 . Morphologically, it is a merger system whose appearance and kinematics resemble the Antennae galaxy Oestlin2015 .

The ionized gas in Haro 11 is assembled by a rich arrangement of structures, such as superbubbles, filaments, arcs, and galactic ionized channels, whose appearances change drastically with velocity. The central star-forming knots and the star-forming dusty arm are the main engines that power the strong mechanical feedback in this galaxy, although with different impact on the ionization structure. Haro 11 appears to leak LyC radiation in many directions. We found evidence of a kpc-scale fragmented superbubble that may have cleared galactic-scale channels in the ISM.

gmw_00006734.name: HZ1
gmw_00006734.overview: HZ1 is a high-redshift star-forming galaxy that exhibits Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:2206.03503]. Observations with ALMA reveal diffuse, extended carbon-two emission [arXiv:2006.09402], though carbon-three emission remains undetected [arXiv:2206.03503].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes HZ1 in paired ALMA datasets, finding lower fluxes in higher-resolution images because surface brightness dimming misses diffuse extended line emission.

quotes.quote: Among the seven galaxies in common with the two ALMA programs, we have analysed only three sources, HZ1, HZ3, and HZ4, since the other targets show a multi-component morphology Carniani:2018 that would lead to a more complex and ambiguous interpretation with respect to current simulations. The two datasets, data calibration, and analysis are presented in App. sec:app_hz . Fig. fig:sim_vs_obs shows the \ fluxes of HZ1, HZ3, and HZ4 inferred from the two individual datasets as a function of the _ beam normalised by the extent of \ emission (D _ source ) estimated from the observations (see App. sec:app_hz for more details).

We notice that the \ measurements obtained from L17, which has lower angular resolution, are systematically higher than those estimated in the higher angular-resolution images of C12. This discrepancy is due to the two different ALMA beams. Indeed in the C12 program the angular resolution of the observations is comparable to the \ size, so increasing the effect of SBD on the flux measurement. Although the significance of the \ detection in C12 is >5 , the sensitivity is not sufficiently high to recover the diffuse and extended emission, whereas in L17 dataset the \ line is barely resolved resulting in a higher flux estimate.

sources.summary: The paper includes HZ1 as an ALMA [C II] high-redshift star-forming galaxy, recovers Ly emission, but finds no C III] detection and therefore reports an upper limit.

quotes.quote: In particular, HZ1 and HZ10 have been observed with ALMA in [ C II ] by , as part of their sample of normal star-forming galaxies at 5<z<6 . Furthermore, the ALMA data has been reanalyzed by , with a focus on a detailed morphological analysis of the [ C II ] emission. Specifically, for HZ10, the [ C II ] line reveals multiple components and the velocity gradient implies a merger scenario. measured [ C II ] luminosity of L_ [ C II ] = 2.5 0.5 10^8 L_ and L_ [ C II ] = 25.1 5.0 10^8 L_ for HZ1 and HZ10, respectively. found the total SFR of HZ1 and HZ10 of SFR =24_ 3 ^ +6 \ M_ \ yr^ 1 and SFR = 169_ 27 ^ +32 \ M_ \ yr^ 1 , respectively.
We recovered the Ly emission detection in HZ1 and HZ10, previously reported in with the DEIMOS spectrograph on the Keck II telescope.

For the other two targets, HZ1 and RXJ 1347-1216, we did not detect C III ] emission at the expected redshifted wavelength and we place a 3 upper limit on the line flux. For HZ1 and RXJ 1347-1216, we estimate the integrated flux upper limit assuming a full-width at half maximum (FWHM) of the C III ] line to be equal to the FWHM of the [ C II ] line, where FWHM_ [ C II ] = 72 11 \ km \ s^ 1 ( 2015Natur.522..455C ) and FWHM_ [ C II ] = 75 25 \ km \ s^ 1 ( 2017ApJ...836L...2B ), respectively.

gmw_00006735.name: HZ4
gmw_00006735.overview: HZ4 is a star-forming main-sequence galaxy at redshift z=5.544 [arXiv:2005.07716, arXiv:2101.05279] that features a regular rotating disk with high turbulence [arXiv:2203.00689]. The system exhibits a diffuse gas halo extending 6 kiloparsecs in radius [arXiv:2212.08083] and shows evidence of gas outflows launched from its central region [arXiv:2101.05279, arXiv:2212.08083].

sources.summary: The paper studies HZ4 as a z=5.544 main-sequence COSMOS galaxy observed in multiple ALMA programmes, including Band 8 data chosen to constrain its infrared SED.

quotes.quote: We focus on four main-sequence galaxies to which we refer to as HZ4 ( z=5.544 ), HZ6 ( z=5.293 ) This galaxy is also known as LBG-1 in RIECHERS14 . , HZ9 ( z=5.541 ), and HZ10 ( z=5.657 ) in the following. These galaxies have been previously discussed by RIECHERS14 and CAPAK15 and are initially spectroscopically selected via and UV absorption lines from a large spectroscopic campaign with Keck/DEIMOS HASINGER18 on the Cosmic Evolution Survey COSMOS, SCOVILLE07 field. All galaxies have been observed with different ALMA programmes covering their rest-frame wavelengths from 100 m to 200 m (see Table tab:observations ).

All four galaxies have been observed recently as part of the ALMA programme 2018.1.00348.S (PI: Faisst) at a frequency of 406.4 GHz (Band 8). This frequency was chosen to optimise the constraints on the infrared SED and to minimise the integration time to reach a S/N of 10 . At that frequency, the Band 8 atmospheric transmission is maximised and going to higher frequencies would increase the integration times significantly. On the other hand, Band 7 observes too low frequencies for robust constraints on the infrared SED together with the archival ALMA observations see Appendix in FAISST17b . In the rest-frame of HZ4 , HZ6 , HZ9 , and HZ10 , Band 8 corresponds to wavelengths of 112.8 m , 117.3 m , 112.9 m , and 111.0 m , respectively.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes HZ4 in paired ALMA datasets, finding lower fluxes in higher-resolution images because surface brightness dimming misses diffuse extended line emission.

quotes.quote: Among the seven galaxies in common with the two ALMA programs, we have analysed only three sources, HZ1, HZ3, and HZ4, since the other targets show a multi-component morphology Carniani:2018 that would lead to a more complex and ambiguous interpretation with respect to current simulations. The two datasets, data calibration, and analysis are presented in App. sec:app_hz . Fig. fig:sim_vs_obs shows the \ fluxes of HZ1, HZ3, and HZ4 inferred from the two individual datasets as a function of the _ beam normalised by the extent of \ emission (D _ source ) estimated from the observations (see App. sec:app_hz for more details).

We notice that the \ measurements obtained from L17, which has lower angular resolution, are systematically higher than those estimated in the higher angular-resolution images of C12. This discrepancy is due to the two different ALMA beams. Indeed in the C12 program the angular resolution of the observations is comparable to the \ size, so increasing the effect of SBD on the flux measurement. Although the significance of the \ detection in C12 is >5 , the sensitivity is not sufficiently high to recover the diffuse and extended emission, whereas in L17 dataset the \ line is barely resolved resulting in a higher flux estimate.

sources.summary: The paper presents kiloparsec-scale ALMA observations of HZ4, a z=5.5 star-forming galaxy, finding ISM constraints, an outflow from the central region, and an extended halo.

quotes.quote: Motivated by these open issues, in this paper we present new \ and dust continuum ALMA observations of HZ4, a typical star-forming galaxy at z=5.5 . The spatial resolution and sensitivity achieved by our observations (see Section sec:data and sec:results ) allow us to study the ISM (Section Sec:cii2fir ), outflow (Section Sec: wind ), and \ extended emission (Section Sec:halo ) properties at a kiloparsec scale level. The analysis of the kinematic properties of \ will be discussed in a forthcoming paper (Paper II; Herrera-Camus et al. in prep.).

For the first time in an individual normal galaxy at this redshift, we find evidence for outflowing gas from the central star-forming region in the direction of the minor-axis of the galaxy. The projected velocity of the outflow is 400 km s ^ 1 , and the neutral gas mass outflow rate is 3-6 times higher than the SFR in the central region. Finally, we detect a diffuse component of \ emission, or halo, that extends beyond the star-forming disk and has a size of 12 kpc in diameter.

sources.summary: The paper cites HZ4 as a z ~5.5 star-forming galaxy with extended line emission forming a 6 kpc halo and broad-wing evidence for outflows reaching the circumgalactic medium.

quotes.quote: Another interesting result came from deep observations of HZ4, a typical star-forming galaxy at z 5.5 herrera2021kiloparsec . These authors found evidence for a emission extending beyond the dust and UV continuum disk, and forming a halo of 6 kpc in radius.

An even more convincing sign of the direct link between halos and outflow activity comes from deep observations of the individual system HZ4 herrera2021kiloparsec . In addition to the spatially extended emission already mentioned in Sec. sec:introduction , from the line broad wings the authors identify the presence of an outflow in two adjacent sub-regions of 2 kpc size, extending from the central disk in the direction of the minor axis. These findings point to the presence of an active outflow, with velocities in the range v 300-400 , launched from the inner galactic regions and reaching out the CGM, that is detected as extended emission.

sources.summary: HZ4 is analyzed as a z=5.5 main-sequence galaxy with ALMA kinematics showing a regular rotating disk, high turbulence, and radial gas transport.

quotes.quote: We have obtained deep, kiloparsec-scale resolution ALMA observations in the 158 m transition of HZ4, a main-sequence galaxy at z=5.5 .

We measure an intrinsic velocity dispersion of _ 0 =65.8^ +2.9 _ 3.3 km s ^ 1 and V_ rot / _ 0 =2.2 . The latter, combined with the smooth, monotonic velocity gradient observed across the galaxy, the alignment between the morphological and kinematic major axis, and a centrally peaked velocity dispersion profile, strongly suggests that \ has a regular rotating disk already in place at z=5.5 , when the Universe was only 1 Gyr old.
We find that only the models that include radial transport of gas are capable of reproducing the high intrinsic velocity dispersion observed in \ (Fig.

sources.summary: The paper gives HZ4 [N II] constraints and interprets its high [C II]/[N II] ratio as evidence for low gas and stellar metallicity.

quotes.quote: We tentatively detect [ N ii ] 205 m emission toward LBG-1 (at 3.4 ), and HZ9 (at 3.1 ), we confidently detect it from HZ10 (at 6 ), and we provide a constraining upper limit for HZ4 (Figures fig:mom_cont fig:spectra ).

On the other hand, we find substantially higher ratios for HZ9, LBG-1 and HZ4, which are only compatible with the ratio observed in local dwarf galaxies (Figure fig:ratio_plot ) and provides further evidence for the diversity of conditions at z>5 already present in this small sample.

Therefore, in analogy to the case of local dwarf galaxies, we interpret our high line ratio measurements and limits for HZ9, LBG-1 and HZ4 as indicative of low gas (and stellar) metallicity, relative to z<5 galaxies of comparable masses ( 10^ 10 M_ ).

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 494057 as a rotator, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 494057 : this object is classified as a rotator, and the [ CII ] emission is much more extended than the optical emission. We re-extracted the [ CII ] spectrum from a smaller region, coincident with the optical emission, but the position of the [ CII ] velocity peak was not affected. There are no signs of multiple components from the images or the spectra.

sources.summary: The paper cites HZ4 as a turbulent disk plus dark matter halo target, with published velocity dispersion and Vmax/sigma values comparable to this work.

quotes.quote: Herrera-Camus:2022 presented the kinematical study of the target HZ4 by modeling the galaxy as a turbulent disk plus a dark matter halo. They found values of the velocity dispersion of _ gas = 65.8 ^ +2.9 _ 3.3 and a value of V_ max / _ gas =2.2 . These results are comparable within the uncertainties to our best-fitting values.

gmw_00006736.name: J0235
gmw_00006736.overview: J0235 is a luminous Lyman-break and Lyman-alpha galaxy at a redshift of approximately 6 [arXiv:1910.10927]. It exhibits strong Lyman-alpha emission without signatures of active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1910.10927], and its carbon-line emission is more spatially extended than its oxygen-line emission [arXiv:2006.09402].

sources.summary: The paper targets J0235-0532 as a z~6 luminous LBG/LAE with strong Ly emission, large Ly luminosity, no broad-line AGN signature, and no detected dust continuum.

quotes.quote: As the first step to understand ISM properties of high redshift galaxies, we select three luminous LBGs, J1211-0118, J0235-0532, and J0217-0208 as targets for ALMA observations (Table tab_prop ). All targets are spectroscopically confirmed with Ly in the SHELLQs project 2018PASJ...70S..35M , and their redshifts, 6.0<z_ Ly <6.3 , are suitable for ALMA Band 6, 7, and 8 observations targeting Cii 158 m, Nii 122 m, and Oiii 88 m, respectively. J1211-0118 shows a weak Ly emission line with a rest-frame Ly equivalent width (EW) of EW^0_ Ly =6.9 0.8 \ . J0235-0532 and J0217-0208 show strong Ly emission lines with EW^0_ Ly =41 2 , and 15 1 \ , respectively.

J0235-0532 and J0217-0208 exhibit large Ly luminosities of L_ Ly >10^ 43 \ erg \ s^ 1 , powerful enough to be associated with AGNs as suggested by 2016ApJ...823...20K at z 2 , while a spectroscopic study by 2018PASJ...70S..15S do not find signatures of AGN activitity (e.g., Civ emission) in such luminous LAEs at z 6-7 . NIR spectroscopy is important for understanding possible AGN activities in J0235-0532 and J0217-0208.
We do not detect dust continuum emission from J0235-0532.

sources.summary: The paper reports that J0235 has co-spatial FIR line detections consistent with prior work, with carbon-line emission more extended than oxygen-line emission.

quotes.quote: For the remaining systems (MACSJ0416-Y1, B14-65666, J0235, J1211, and J0217) for which co-spatial \ and \ emissions have been already reported in the literature Tamura:2019, Hashimoto:2019,Harikane:2019, Bakx:2020 , we have found results consistent with previous works. In Table tab:summary we list the \ and \ luminosities. Since for these galaxies the two FIR lines have been detected with high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR >8 ), we take advantage of these observations to investigate the extent of the FIR lines, as shown in Fig. fig:size (and Table tab:appendix ). We notice that \ is systematically larger than the \ and its extent is about two times larger than that of oxygen line. This is consistent with recent simulations by where the \ is concentrated in a compact region of 0.85 kpc, while the \ arise from a more extended area with a radius of 1.54 kpc.

gmw_00006737.name: NGC 3801
gmw_00006737.overview: NGC 3801 is a low-power Fanaroff-Riley type-I radio galaxy [arXiv:1401.5312, arXiv:1112.5221] located within a group of ten galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256]. It features a large-scale neutral hydrogen disc [arXiv:1112.5221], likely star-forming regions [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256], and a complex, recently acquired dust distribution [arXiv:1402.5545] that includes a warped main dust lane [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 3801 as a young low-power FR I radio galaxy whose compact lobes, spectral break, CRe ages, X-ray shells, dust lanes, and disturbed ISM indicate jet-driven feedback.

quotes.quote: We use VLA and BIMA observations to study the radio continuum emission of NGC 3801 between 1.4 and 112.4 GHz . We find a prominent spectral break at 10 GHz, where the spectrum steepens as expected from cosmic-ray electron (CRe) ageing. Using the equipartition magnetic field and fitting JP models locally we create a spatially resolved map of the spectral age of the CRe population. The spectral age of _ int =2.0 0.2 Myr agrees within a factor of two with the dynamical age of the expanding X-ray emitting shells. The spectral age varies only little across the lobes, requiring an effective mixing process of the CRe such as a convective backflow of magnetized plasma.

In this paper, we present new RC observations of the low-power FR I object NGC 3801. The radio lobes in this galaxy are small ( 10 kpc ) and buried within the ISM of the S0/a host galaxy, which shows a disturbed morphology. It has two prominent dust lanes, one along the minor axis and another roughly perpendicular extending from the centre to the eastern edge heckman_86a .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3801 as a group galaxy with an extensive warped dust lane, likely star-forming knots, HI, and a double-lobed radio structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 3801 NGC 3801 is inside a group of ten galaxies (Garcia 1993). It has a very extended and complicated dust distribution. There is a main dust lane ( 4.4 kpc/ 21'' ) perpendicular to the major axis of the galaxy with a warped and stranded structure. A second filamentary dust distribution extends roughly perpendicular to the east-side of the main dust lane while on the west side there are about six thinner parallel filaments also roughly perpendicular to the main dust lane. This structure spans 60'' (12.6 kpc) and is visible on both the PC and WF .

The brightest `knots' coincide with slightly brighter small spots in the V band image and are likely to be star forming regions. HI is detected in emission (Heckman 1983) and also in absorption with an estimated mass of 2.1 10^9 (Duprie 1996). Radio maps by Jenkins (1982) and Xu (1999a) show a warped double-lobed profile roughly perpendicular to the main dust lane.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 3801 as a nearby FR-I radio galaxy with a large rotating H I disc, cold gas, radio jets, and evidence for merger or interaction-driven evolution.

quotes.quote: We present observations of a large-scale disc of neutral hydrogen ( ) in the nearby Fanaroff Riley type-I radio galaxy NGC 3801 with the Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope. The \ disc (34 kpc in diameter and with M_ HI = 1.3 10^ 9 M_ ) is aligned with the radio jet axis. This makes NGC 3801 an ideal system for investigating the evolution of a small radio source through its host galaxy's cold ISM.

We argue that the formation history of the large-scale \ disc is in agreement with earlier speculation that NGC 3801 was involved in a past gas-rich galaxy-galaxy merger (although other formation histories are discussed). The fact that NGC 3801 is located in an environment of several rich companions, and shows indications of ongoing interaction with the nearby companion NGC 3802, strengthens this possibility.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 3801 has a dim NLR-like component probably tied to host structure, no clumpy-torus evidence, and ambiguous warm nuclear emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 3801 The best fit for NGC 3801 requires a NLR component, however it is very dim so we suspect it is likely either a fitting artifact or possibly associated with a large-scale structure in the host galaxy. A likely candidate for this component from the host is the large dust lane visible near the AGN in HST images by . The forced-torus fit is significantly worse, with a BIC of 453.7 compared to the best-fit BIC of 423.5. We also show an extincted galaxy-only fit with a BIC of 437.3 in Figure fig:SED-NGC3801-doae as a comparison since the dust lane which is a candidate thermal source would also provide foreground extinction on the core. Overall, we do not find evidence for nuclear obscuration in the form of a clumpy torus and our results are ambiguous for any other warm thermal component in the nucleus itself due to likely emission from the host galaxy. detected a flat-spectrum non-thermal core in NGC 3801 in 3 mm images from the Berkeley-Illinois-Maryland Association millimeter-wave array.

sources.summary: The paper reports strong X-ray shocked shells around NGC 3801 radio lobes, with Mach 3-6 expansion and shell energy comparable to the host ISM, making shock heating the dominant energy-transfer mechanism.

quotes.quote: Here we discuss X-ray detections of strong shocks in nearby galaxies, including a new detection of shocked gas around both lobes of the nearby radio galaxy NGC 3801 NGC 3801 with inferred Mach numbers of 3 -- 6 and a total injected energy comparable to the thermal energy of the ISM within 11 kpc.

Our recent Chandra observations of 801 revealed a second definite example of strong shocks produced by a small FRI source on galaxy scales. Fig 3801 shows the Chandra detected emission from 801, which traces well the outer edges of the radio lobes.

The total energy is also equivalent to the thermal energy of the ISM within 11 kpc (or 25 percent of the thermal energy within 30 kpc). Shock heating is therefore the dominant energy transfer mechanism during this phase of radio-source activity, and will have dramatic long term effects: part or all of the ISM may be expelled from the galaxy, and the entropy of the gas will be permanently increased.

sources.summary: J1140+1743 is treated as a red-spectrum Seyfert 2 in an S0 host with a large dust lane, weak lines, symmetric jets, and a forming disk.

quotes.quote: J1140+1743 This is a Seyfert 2 galaxy hosted in an S0 with a large-scale dust lane . Its spectrum is extremely red, with weak emission lines. We cannot exclude that the AGN is in its last phase before reaching a quiescent state . The source has symmetric jets, and it appears to be forming a disk.

sources.summary: The paper highlights NGC 3801 as having recently acquired dust, with complex morphology, a larger relative grain size, and elevated R_V before the dust settled smoothly.

quotes.quote: On the other hand if the dust is acquired recently, it will lack regular dust morphology as in the case of NGC 3801 . Among our sample galaxies NGC 3801 shows considerably larger relative grain size (1.47) and larger value of R_ V .

The results of support that NGC 3801 acquired dust in the recent past and did not have enough time to settle it into a smooth, regular morphology, which may result in the observed larger grain size and R_ V value, as suggested by .

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 3801 as a comparison galaxy-scale jet, citing its spectral index, roughly 10 kpc radio emission, and X-ray evidence for jets driving ISM shocks.

quotes.quote: This is consistent with the values seen in more powerful radio-galaxy populations (e,g, the 3CRR sample of Laing1983BrightGalaxies , with a typical spectral index of = -0.76 ), as well as the value of 0.6 0.1 found by Heesen2014ThePopulation when studying a different GSJ, NGC 3801, and the value of 0.63 found by Sabater2019TheOn when analysing the wider LoTSS AGN sample.

The situation for at least some of our GSJ may be similar to NGC 3801 which, whilst more luminous than our sources, has radio emission about 10 kpc in size and can be considered a GSJ Heesen2014ThePopulation . Using X-ray data Croston2007Shock3801 found that the jets of NGC 3801 were driving shocks into the host ISM.

gmw_00006738.name: NGC 3802
gmw_00006738.overview: NGC 3802 is a gas-rich disk galaxy classified as either an S0 or a spiral galaxy [arXiv:1112.5221]. It is a close companion to NGC 3801, and the two galaxies exhibit an ongoing interaction indicated by an asymmetric distribution of neutral hydrogen and an apparent gas bridge [arXiv:1112.5221].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3802 as the gas-rich companion to NGC 3801, with asymmetric H I and a bridge or warp suggesting ongoing interaction between the two galaxies.

quotes.quote: The close companion NGC 3802 is a disc-galaxy (S0 or spiral) approximately 33 kpc north of NGC 3801. NGC 3802 contains M_ HI 3.9 10^ 7 M_ of \ gas. The \ gas has an asymmetric distribution across NGC 3802, peaking in the western part of the disc and continuing on the eastern side, covering a total velocity range of 400 \ (see Fig. fig:HIdisc right for more details).

In fact, Figs. fig:HIdisc (right) and fig:HIchanmaps show that there is an apparent bridge or warp of \ gas stretching from the north-western part of the \ disc of NGC 3801 towards the western part of the disc of NGC 3802. This \ feature is too faint to be identified in the total intensity image of Fig. fig:HIdisc (left) . The asymmetric \ distribution in companion galaxy NGC 3802 and the apparent \ bridge in between NGC 3801 and NGC 3802 suggests that the NGC 3801/3802 system is in ongoing interaction.

gmw_00006739.name: Mrk 50
gmw_00006739.overview: Mrk 50 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1205.3789] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1205.3789, arXiv:1809.05113]. Its broad-line region is structured as a nearly face-on thick disk [arXiv:1205.3789] and exhibits signatures of outflowing gas trajectories [arXiv:1909.01395].

sources.summary: The paper models Mrk 50 broad-line-region data, finding revised modeling assumptions, good line-profile fits, captured large-scale flux variability, and a radial emission distribution.

quotes.quote: Mrk 50 The BLR in \ was previously modeled by pancoast12 using the same LAMP 2011 data and an earlier version of the model used in this paper. Their model did not include the parameters or which help introduce asymmetries in the broad-line profile, and the dynamics component did not allow for macroturbulent velocities or the possibility of unbound inflowing or outflowing gas. We also include an additional narrow-line component in the model and leave the AGN redshift as a free parameter.

The model for \ fits the shape of the \ emission line very well, as shown in Figure fig:display_all . The large-scale fluctuations in the integrated \ flux are well captured, but the small peak in flux in the first few epochs is not recovered. The radial distribution of \ emission in \ is between Gaussian and exponential, with a shape parameter = .

sources.summary: The paper models Mrk 50 reverberation data, finding a nearly face-on thick BLR disk, constraining its geometry and inclination, and deriving an independent black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: We present dynamical modeling of the broad line region (BLR) in the Seyfert 1 galaxy Mrk 50 using reverberation mapping data taken as part of the Lick AGN Monitoring Project (LAMP) 2011. We model the reverberation mapping data directly, constraining the geometry and kinematics of the BLR, as well as deriving a black hole mass estimate that does not depend on a normalizing factor or virial coefficient. We find that the geometry of the BLR in Mrk 50 is a nearly face-on thick disk, with a mean radius of 9.6^ +1.2 _ 0.9 light days, a width of the BLR of 6.9^ +1.2 _ 1.1 light days, and a disk opening angle of 25 10 degrees above the plane.
We also constrain the inclination angle to be 9^ +7 _ 5 degrees, close to face-on. Finally, the black hole mass of Mrk 50 is inferred to be _ 10 ( / M_ )=7.57^ +0.44 _ 0.27 .

sources.summary: The paper models Mrk 50 and constrains inclination, outflow signatures, angular/radial BLR parameters, and marginal transparency/orbit parameters consistent with full light-curve results.

quotes.quote: Mrk 50 Some of the parameters can be constrained and agree within the errors with the full light-curve result: we find = mrk50_inc degrees, = mrk50_kappa , \ is constrained to be > 0.5 i.e. signatures of outflowing trajectories, = mrk50_thetae degrees and = mrk50_rmin light-days. The parameter \ is somewhat constrained to mrk50_fellip . Although the posterior probability distribution for \ is broad, it indicates that scenarios where 1.0, i.e. no gas is inflowing/outflowing, are not favoured. The parameter is also marginally constrained to = mrk50_eps with a broad posterior probability distribution. Both and nevertheless agree with the full light-curve results within the uncertainties. The remaining parameters have very broad posterior probability distributions (Fig. posterior_mrk50 ), and cannot be accurately constrained.

gmw_00006740.name: PLCK G045.1 + 61.1
gmw_00006740.overview: PLCK G045.1 + 61.1 is a gravitationally lensed starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z=3.427 [arXiv:1610.01169]. It features robust blue and red spectral components [arXiv:1811.11215], with each component containing approximately half of the total gas mass [arXiv:1811.11215].

sources.summary: PLCK G045.1+61.1 shows robust blue and red spectral components; both modeled CO SLEDs peak near J=4-5 and each component holds about half the gas mass.

quotes.quote: Seven out of the 11 GEMS exhibit single Gaussian profiles and we only use a double Gaussian for PLCK G045.1+61.1, PLCK G092.5+42.9, PLCK G145.2+50.9, and PLCK G244.8+54.9, where this additional spectral component is robustly detected (see Figures fig:lines1 , fig:lines2 , fig:lines3 , and fig:lines4 ).
